candidate_problem: The paper identifies a gap in mapping specific multi-agent coordination architectures to predictable failure modes (MAST taxonomy), specifically noting that sequential pipelines are prone to task-verification gaps (FM-3) due to lack of architectural verification opportunities.
evidence_spans: The MAST taxonomy of Cemri et al. ( 2025 ) identifies three top-level failure clusters: system-design issues, inter-agent misalignment, and task-verification gaps.
a configuration with strict stage ordering and no architectural opportunity for upstream verification (sequential pipeline) is a plausible breeding ground for FM-3.

Predicting which of these MAST failure modes a particular configuration is most likely to exhibit from the configuration’s specification alone —the goal articulated in Section 2.1 —appears feasible at the resolution of this experiment.
problem_evidence_spans: a configuration with strict stage ordering and no architectural opportunity for upstream verification (sequential pipeline) is a plausible breeding ground for FM-3.
feasibility_evidence_spans: All code, data, and infrastructure for this paper are publicly released and pinned to specific commits: • Harness, configurations, analysis: https://github.com/ForesightFlow/coordination-experiment
auto_research_experiment: Using the provided 'coordination-experiment' harness, scale the evaluation of the 'sequential pipeline' and 'consensus alignment' architectures from n=100 to n=500 markets. Specifically, measure the frequency of FM-3 (task-verification gaps) in the sequential pipeline versus the baseline consensus model using the Murphy decomposition and trace analysis.
available_data_or_benchmark: Polymarket binary markets and the Foresight Arena live agent harness.
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/ForesightFlow/datasets/tree/main/coordination-traces-100
scientific_hypothesis: Increasing the sample size to n=500 will statistically confirm that sequential pipeline architectures exhibit a significantly higher rate of FM-3 (task-verification) failures compared to architectures with built-in verification layers.
expected_metric: Frequency of FM-3 failure signatures in execution traces.
success_threshold: Statistically significant difference (p < 0.05 after Bonferroni correction) in FM-3 rates between sequential and consensus architectures.
failure_condition: The FM-3 failure rate does not significantly differ between architectures even at n=500, suggesting the failure is model-bound rather than architecture-bound.
first_24h_experiment: Clone the repository, verify the harness runs on a subset of 10 new Polymarket IDs, and confirm the trace-logger correctly flags 'inadequate output checking' (FM-3).
specific_intervention: Scaling the sample size to n=500 and isolating the 'sequential pipeline' architecture to measure FM-3 frequency.
baseline: The 'consensus alignment' architecture (reported as having lower FM-3 risk but higher FM-2 risk).
story_angle: Moving from 'engineering convenience' to 'architectural science' by proving that how we connect agents deterministically creates specific failure types.

candidate_problem: Spurious divergence in image-derived glymphatic velocity fields violating conservation of mass
evidence_spans: Measurement and reconstruction errors in image-derived velocity fields commonly manifest as spurious divergence of the velocity field, leading to the violation of the conservation of mass.
reconstructed velocity fields frequently reflect experimental artifacts rather than the underlying physics.
problem_evidence_spans: Measurement and reconstruction errors in image-derived velocity fields commonly manifest as spurious divergence of the velocity field, leading to the violation of the conservation of mass.
feasibility_evidence_spans: In preclinical mouse brain models—the focus of the present work—glymphatic transport is typically captured via contrast-enhanced MRI (CE-MRI)
The resulting modified advection-diffusion equation is solved using an immersed finite element method within an isogeometric analysis (IGA) framework.
auto_research_experiment: Implement the Hughes-Wells (HW) additive decomposition as a physics-based regularizer within a Newton-based inverse solver to recover solenoidal velocity fields from noisy CE-MRI tracer data.
available_data_or_benchmark: Preclinical contrast-enhanced MRI (CE-MRI) time-series of tracer transport in mouse brains.
public_training_or_eval_data: The 'Glymphatic MRI' dataset or similar open-access preclinical neuroimaging repositories (e.g., ADNI or specialized mouse glymphatic datasets like those from Mestre et al. 2018).
scientific_hypothesis: Enforcing a divergence-free constraint via the Hughes-Wells formulation in an inverse setting will significantly reduce tracer conservation errors compared to unconstrained finite-difference velocity extraction.
expected_metric: Conservation error (L2 norm of the divergence of the velocity field) and tracer concentration RMSE.
success_threshold: Divergence of the reconstructed field < 1e-6 and tracer mass conservation error < 1% over the simulation duration.
failure_condition: The Newton solver fails to converge or the corrective velocity component dominates the physical signal, leading to non-physiological transport patterns.
first_24h_experiment: Generate a synthetic 3D tracer transport dataset with known solenoidal velocity and added Gaussian noise; attempt to recover the velocity field using the HW-modified inverse solver vs. a standard unconstrained solver.
specific_intervention: Hughes-Wells (HW) additive velocity decomposition (u = u_bar + grad(phi)) applied as a regularizer in the inverse advection-diffusion problem.
baseline: Standard unconstrained inverse reconstruction or direct finite-difference estimation from voxelized intensity changes.
story_angle: By treating the brain's waste-clearance system as a fluid dynamics problem with strict conservation laws, we can finally see the 'invisible' currents that flush out Alzheimer's-linked proteins.

candidate_problem: The paper identifies a gap in predicting which specific MAST (Multi-Agent Systems Taxonomy) failure modes (e.g., FM-2 communication breakdown vs FM-3 verification gaps) will emerge from a given coordination configuration's specification alone.
evidence_spans: Predicting which of these MAST failure modes a particular configuration is most likely to exhibit from the configuration’s specification alone —the goal articulated in Section 2.1 —appears feasible at the resolution of this experiment.
problem_evidence_spans: Predicting which of these MAST failure modes a particular configuration is most likely to exhibit from the configuration’s specification alone —the goal articulated in Section 2.1 —appears feasible at the resolution of this experiment.

auto_research_experiment: Reproduce the five coordination configurations (sequential, peer-critique, consensus, etc.) using the provided harness. Scale the evaluation to n=500 markets as suggested in the paper's power analysis. Measure the frequency of MAST failure modes (FM-2 vs FM-3) across configurations to verify if 'strict stage ordering' consistently triggers FM-3 and 'all-to-all communication' triggers FM-2.
available_data_or_benchmark: Polymarket binary markets and the Foresight Arena live-agent harness.
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/ForesightFlow/datasets/tree/main/coordination-traces-100
scientific_hypothesis: Coordination configurations with strict stage ordering and no architectural verification (sequential pipelines) exhibit a significantly higher ratio of MAST FM-3 (verification gaps) than FM-2 (misalignment) compared to all-to-all peer-critique configurations.
expected_metric: Ratio of FM-3 to FM-2 failure occurrences per configuration.
success_threshold: A statistically significant (p < 0.05) difference in failure mode distribution between sequential and peer-critique architectures at n=500.
failure_condition: The distribution of failure modes is uniform across architectures or the sample size increase does not resolve the contrasts.
first_24h_experiment: Clone the coordination-experiment repo, verify the environment, and run the sequential and peer-critique configurations on a 20-market subset to categorize failures using the MAST taxonomy.
specific_intervention: Scaling the sample size to n=500 and applying the MAST failure mode classification to the resulting traces.
baseline: The n=100 results and the three qualitative case studies (Markets 66, 32, 92) reported in the paper.
story_angle: By scaling the author's own experimental framework, we can move from qualitative 'plausible breeding grounds' for agent failure to a predictive law of multi-agent architecture.

candidate_problem: Scale mismatch between discrete microscopic transport pathways and coarse voxel-averaged clinical imaging
evidence_spans: reduced-order or homogenized approximations Solheim et al. [ 2025 ] , which attenuate local velocity peaks and create scale mismatch between the discrete microscopic pathways of fluid transport and the coarse, voxel-averaged observations captured by clinical imaging
problem_evidence_spans: attenuate local velocity peaks and create scale mismatch between the discrete microscopic pathways of fluid transport and the coarse, voxel-averaged observations captured by clinical imaging
feasibility_evidence_spans: IGA facilitates the projection of these high-dimensional image data and transport fields onto B-spline basis functions, naturally providing regularization and smoothing.
auto_research_experiment: Compare B-spline basis function order (p=2 vs p=4) in an IGA framework to determine the minimum resolution required to preserve local velocity peaks in the perivascular space (PVS) without increasing noise sensitivity.
available_data_or_benchmark: High-resolution micro-CT or synchrotron-based imaging of mouse vasculature paired with CE-MRI.
public_training_or_eval_data: The 'Mouse Brain Vasculature' dataset (e.g., from the Allen Institute or similar high-res vascular atlases).
scientific_hypothesis: Higher-order B-spline basis functions in an immersed IGA framework can recover sub-voxel velocity peaks in perivascular spaces that are lost in standard voxel-based finite element methods.
expected_metric: Peak Velocity Recovery Ratio (PVRR) compared to ground truth high-res simulations.
success_threshold: Recovery of >80% of local velocity peaks within the PVS at standard MRI resolutions (approx 100um).
failure_condition: High-order splines introduce oscillatory artifacts (Runge's phenomenon) that destabilize the advection-diffusion solver.
first_24h_experiment: Create a 1D synthetic signal with sharp peaks representing PVS flow; compare B-spline interpolation vs. linear interpolation in the presence of 5% noise.
specific_intervention: High-order B-spline basis functions (p >= 3) in an Isogeometric Analysis (IGA) framework.
baseline: Standard linear Finite Element Method (FEM) on a voxel-conforming mesh.
story_angle: Standard brain scans are too blurry to see the tiny 'pipes' that clean the brain; new mathematical 'lenses' using B-splines might sharpen our view without needing better hardware.

candidate_problem: The instability of symbolic coding thresholds in multi-channel signaling games leads to 'strategic collapse' where agents fail to stabilize a shared code.
evidence_spans: coding innovations may be vulnerable to strategic collapse due to multichannel failures, thus increasing the risk of systemic fragility and evolutionary dead ends.

Our game-theoretic analysis is intended to shift the interpretation of “hard steps” from elusive improbability to equilibrium structure, arguing that algorithmic bottlenecks correspond to unstable equilibria where signaling, coordination, and control must tightly align.
problem_evidence_spans: defection — whether by minimizing signaling effort or exploiting shared resources — is tempting.
These dynamics must not only stabilize themselves, but also invent new information architectures that unlock further evolutionary phase spaces
feasibility_evidence_spans: The emergence of technological society can be modeled as an iterated or evolutionary n-player Prisoner’s Dilemma (Commons Dilemma)
Table 1: Conceptual mapping between genetic and linguistic coding systems.
auto_research_experiment: Simulate an evolutionary multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) environment where agents must develop a shared symbolic mapping (a 'code') to solve a coordination task. Introduce 'signaling cost' and 'multi-channel noise'. Test if the population collapses into a non-communicative state (stagnation) when the complexity of the task requires a 'coding threshold' (mapping discrete symbols to abstract functions) versus simple indexical signaling.
available_data_or_benchmark: The 'Emergent Communication' (EmerComms) benchmarks in MARL, specifically the 'Lewis Signaling Games' or 'Referential Games' frameworks.
public_training_or_eval_data: EGG (Emergence of Language in Games) toolkit by Facebook Research.
scientific_hypothesis: The transition from indexical signaling to symbolic coding is an unstable equilibrium; increasing the number of signaling channels or the cost of high-fidelity translation will cause a population to revert to low-complexity signaling (the 'Great Filter' bottleneck) unless a specific 'JOOTS' (Jump Out Of The System) mechanism is present.
expected_metric: Code Stability Index (CSI) and Task Success Rate.
success_threshold: CSI > 0.8 in high-noise environments with symbolic mapping.
failure_condition: Agents converge to a 'babbling' equilibrium where symbols have no consistent functional mapping (CSI < 0.2).
first_24h_experiment: Set up a standard Lewis Game using the EGG toolkit. Introduce a 'mutation' rate in the symbol-to-meaning mapping and a 'metabolic cost' for using more than 5 distinct symbols. Measure the population's ability to maintain a 10-symbol lexicon over 1000 generations.
specific_intervention: Introduce a 'translation machinery' cost (simulating the ribosome or grammar) that must be paid collectively to unlock higher-order rewards.
baseline: Standard Sender-Receiver game without symbolic mapping costs (purely indexical).
metric: Topographic Similarity (correlation between symbol distance and meaning distance).
story_angle: The 'Great Filter' might not be a lack of life, but a mathematical trap where complex communication systems collapse under their own strategic weight before they can stabilize.

candidate_problem: Infeasibility of joint goal-obfuscation and tracking under tight envelope constraints
evidence_spans: It is demonstrated that large uncertainties of the RBPF representation of the the agent’s state can render the joint mission infeasible in the presence of tight tracking envelope constraints.
Further study of convergence properties of the RBPF is necessary to produce results establishing the infeasibility of obfuscation if practical stability of tracking errors is enforced.
problem_evidence_spans: Further study of convergence properties of the RBPF is necessary to produce results establishing the infeasibility of obfuscation if practical stability of tracking errors is enforced.
feasibility_evidence_spans: The work of Dragan et al. ( 2013 ) showed that trajectories optimizing a task cost are inherently legible
Prior work Wang et al. ( 2025b ) formalized and quantified this legibility for continuous-state systems... maintaining N >> 0 weighted particles... via a Rao-Blackwellized particle filter (RBPF)
auto_research_experiment: Simulate a goal-directed agent (e.g., a 2D quadrotor or mobile robot) using the proposed PCBF controller against a standard RBPF adversary. Systematically tighten the tracking error envelope (rho) and measure the time-to-convergence of the adversary's posterior belief. Identify the critical rho value where the PCBF becomes infeasible (QP solver failure).
available_data_or_benchmark: The RBPF adversary framework from Wang et al. (2025b) and the standard 2D navigation benchmarks used in Dragan et al. (2013).
public_training_or_eval_data: Open-source robotics simulators like PyBullet or OpenAI Gym (specifically navigation tasks).
scientific_hypothesis: There exists a phase transition in the tracking error bound below which any stabilizing controller must reveal the goal state to a Bayesian observer within a finite time horizon.
expected_metric: Adversary Posterior Entropy / Time to Goal
success_threshold: Identify a statistically significant correlation (p < 0.01) between tracking precision and goal-inference speed across 1000+ simulated trajectories.
failure_condition: The agent can maintain perfect tracking while keeping adversary entropy high (indicating the trade-off is not fundamental or the adversary is weak).
first_24h_experiment: Implement a 2D point-mass simulation with a Lyapunov-based tracking controller and a Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter observer. Log the KL-divergence of the observer's belief as the tracking gain is increased.
specific_intervention: Vary the tracking envelope parameter (rho) and the interpolation parameter (mu) to observe the impact on the RBPF's KL-divergence growth.
baseline: Standard Lyapunov-based tracking controller (no obfuscation) and the Dragan et al. (2013) legibility-optimized controller.
metric: KL-divergence between adversary belief and true goal location.
story_angle: As autonomous systems become more predictable to observers, we must find the mathematical limit where 'being safe' and 'being private' become mutually exclusive.

candidate_problem: First-order isotropic evolutionary algorithms (like GO-EA or basic SGA) struggle to navigate the ill-conditioned, stiff loss landscapes of Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs).
evidence_spans: Because PINNs embed partial differential equations directly into the loss function, the resulting loss landscape is notoriously ill-conditioned and riddled with degenerate saddle points... First-order isotropic methods (like basic SGA) or even diagonal methods (like RMSProp) struggle profoundly in such stiff landscapes.

By defining V g via L-BFGS or quasi-Newton pre-conditioning and coupling it with the DLS noise relation, an evolutionary simulation is now available that brings exactly the anisotropic variance these ill-conditioned landscapes demand.
problem_evidence_spans: First-order isotropic methods (like basic SGA) or even diagonal methods (like RMSProp) struggle profoundly in such stiff landscapes because the simulated dynamics cannot reliably follow the sharp fitness ridges
feasibility_evidence_spans: utilizing quasi-Newton pre-conditioning (e.g., L-BFGS) has been empirically proven to reduce the magnitude of the eigenvalues and the condition number of the PINN Hessian by a factor of 1000 or more
auto_research_experiment: Apply the DLS framework with L-BFGS pre-conditioning and DLS noise to train a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) on a standard PDE (e.g., Burgers' equation) and compare convergence rates against isotropic evolutionary algorithms.
available_data_or_benchmark: Burgers' equation or Navier-Stokes PDE residuals for PINN training.
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/danielgrimmer/adam-dls
scientific_hypothesis: Anisotropic DLS (using L-BFGS pre-conditioning) will converge significantly faster than isotropic evolutionary methods on PINN landscapes by effectively following sharp fitness ridges while maintaining evolutionary fidelity.
expected_metric: PDE residual error (MSE) vs. generation count.
success_threshold: DLS with L-BFGS pre-conditioning reaches a residual error of 1e-4 in 10x fewer generations than isotropic SGA.
failure_condition: The addition of DLS noise to the L-BFGS pre-conditioned gradient destabilizes the PINN training, leading to divergence or slower convergence than the deterministic baseline.
first_24h_experiment: Set up a simple PINN for Burgers' equation and implement a DLS-certified L-BFGS optimizer (L-BFGS gradient + DLS noise) to observe initial convergence behavior.
specific_intervention: Integrate L-BFGS pre-conditioning into the DLS framework by setting the variance schedule V_g to the inverse Hessian approximation and adding the required DLS noise term.
baseline: Isotropic Stochastic Gradient Ascent (SGA) or GO-EA.
metric: Mean Squared Error (MSE) of the PDE residual.
story_angle: Using evolutionary first principles to solve the 'stiffness' problem in Physics-Informed Neural Networks.

candidate_problem: Lack of hardware validation for PCBF-based intent privacy
evidence_spans: Further work developing numerical and hardware experiments is necessary for validation.
problem_evidence_spans: Further work developing numerical and hardware experiments is necessary for validation.
feasibility_evidence_spans: The framework has been deployed across a wide range of domains including legged locomotion, adaptive cruise control, and multi-robot coordination Ames et al. ( 2019 ) .
auto_research_experiment: Deploy the PCBF controller on a physical TurtleBot3 or similar low-cost mobile robot. Use an external camera (OptiTrack or similar) to act as the 'adversary' observer. Measure if the theoretical privacy-in-probability guarantees hold under real-world sensor noise.
available_data_or_benchmark: TurtleBot3 ROS2 navigation stack.
public_training_or_eval_data: Robot Operating System (ROS) standard navigation packages and datasets.
scientific_hypothesis: Real-world sensor noise and actuation jitter act as a natural obfuscator, potentially relaxing the theoretical requirements for the PCBF controller.
expected_metric: Empirical Failure Probability (delta_f)
success_threshold: The empirical rate of goal-inference by the adversary matches or is lower than the theoretical bound derived in Theorem 2.
failure_condition: Hardware latency causes the PCBF quadratic program to become unsolvable in real-time (frequency < 10Hz).
first_24h_experiment: Set up a ROS2 simulation (Gazebo) of a mobile robot and implement the RBPF observer to see if it can handle simulated sensor noise before moving to hardware.
specific_intervention: Introduce varying levels of Gaussian and salt-and-pepper noise to the robot's position measurements provided to the adversary.
baseline: Nominal CLF-CBF controller without the privacy-preserving interpolation.
metric: Success rate of the adversary in identifying the goal within the first 50% of the trajectory.
story_angle: Bridging the gap between the elegant math of Probabilistic Control Barrier Functions and the messy reality of robot hardware.

candidate_problem: Euclidean distance-based connectivity in dynamic occupancy models fails to account for landscape resistance and barriers, leading to biased environmental inference for species like the Eurasian otter.
evidence_spans: Integrating a resistance-based formulation of connectivity would be particularly relevant for species like the otter which primarily disperse along rivers and coastlines
Euclidean distance is often replaced in connectivity analyses by functional metrics like least-cost path

environmental effects appeared comparatively moderate in our case (Fig. 4), with a high and relatively homogeneous installation probability across the country. This suggests that, at least in France, part of the observed association between otter presence and environmental variables arises from dispersal history rather than habitat selection.
problem_evidence_spans: The model can be improved by incorporating more functional definitions of connectivity. Indeed, we defined connectivity as a function of Euclidean distance between sites.
dispersal is often constrained by anthropogenic or natural barriers that Euclidean distance alone cannot capture.
feasibility_evidence_spans: we introduce a dispersal-pressure formulation that provides a flexible and ecologically interpretable representation of the colonization process, and develop a sparse distance matrix implementation that enables application to large study areas comprising several thousand sites.
auto_research_experiment: Replace the Euclidean distance matrix in the paper's sparse implementation with a Least-Cost Path (LCP) distance matrix derived from a river-network resistance surface for the Eurasian otter (Lutra lutra) dataset. Compare the model fit (WAIC) and the magnitude of environmental coefficients (e.g., cropland, riparian vegetation) between the Euclidean and LCP versions.
available_data_or_benchmark: The paper uses French national monitoring data for Eurasian otters (Lutra lutra) and Grey wolves (Canis lupus).
public_training_or_eval_data: The French National Biodiversity Observatory (ONB) and the French Biodiversity Agency (OFB) provide species occurrence data; environmental layers (Corine Land Cover) are public.
scientific_hypothesis: Incorporating river-network-based functional connectivity (Least-Cost Path) will significantly increase the estimated effect size of environmental covariates (habitat selection) for otters by correctly attributing historical colonization lags to landscape resistance rather than environmental suitability.
expected_metric: Widely Applicable Information Criterion (WAIC)
success_threshold: A reduction in WAIC of >10 units and a statistically significant change (non-overlapping 95% CI) in the 'cropland' or 'riparian' coefficient estimates compared to the Euclidean baseline.
failure_condition: The LCP model fails to converge or shows no improvement in WAIC, suggesting Euclidean distance is a sufficient proxy for dispersal in this specific landscape.
first_24h_experiment: Download the Corine Land Cover and French river network (BD Carthage). Generate a 5km x 5km grid resistance surface where river pixels have low resistance and non-river pixels have high resistance. Compute a sparse LCP distance matrix for the first 1000 sites.
specific_intervention: Substitute the Euclidean distance calculation in the 'dispersal-pressure' kernel with a Least-Cost Path distance calculated over a resistance surface.
baseline: The paper's original Euclidean distance-based dynamic occupancy model.
metric: Posterior mean and 95% Credible Intervals of environmental coefficients (alpha_xi) and model deviance.
story_angle: Moving beyond 'as the crow flies'—how accounting for river networks reveals the true habitat needs of recovering species.

candidate_problem: Lack of absolute Time-of-Flight (ToF) estimation in current 60 GHz radio channel datasets prevents fine-grained ranging and high-precision SLAM.
evidence_spans: Synchronizing transmitter and receiver clocks would enable absolute Time-of-Flight estimation, unlocking finer-grained ranging and SLAM capabilities.
problem_evidence_spans: Synchronizing transmitter and receiver clocks would enable absolute Time-of-Flight estimation, unlocking finer-grained ranging and SLAM capabilities.
feasibility_evidence_spans: The dataset was collected using a fully programmable RFSoC-based platform integrated with a commercial 60 GHz phased-array front-end
We have acquired a dense dataset for indoor positioning, beam management and digital twin [ 10 ] reconstruction which has been made available for the scientific community [ 13 ]
auto_research_experiment: Develop a synthetic clock-offset correction algorithm that uses the known geometry of the 350 measurement points to 'back-calculate' the clock drift and convert the existing relative phase/timing data into pseudo-absolute ToF for improved SLAM.
available_data_or_benchmark: The 60 GHz Radio Channel Dataset (350 measurement points, 63x63 beamspace).
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/scazzoli/60GHz-RFSoC-Dataset (referenced as [13] in paper)
scientific_hypothesis: A geometry-aware synchronization algorithm can recover absolute ToF from unsynchronized RFSoC captures by exploiting the spatial consistency of the 350-point grid.
expected_metric: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) in ranging.
success_threshold: MAE < 10cm (standard for high-precision indoor mmWave).
failure_condition: Clock jitter in the RFSoC hardware exceeds the chirp duration's stability, making phase recovery impossible.
first_24h_experiment: Download the dataset and analyze the phase stability of the 64 quasi-orthogonal chirps across consecutive captures at a single fixed position to quantify the unsynchronized drift.
specific_intervention: Apply a Least-Squares optimization to estimate the clock offset at each of the 350 grid points using the known physical distance to the TX.
baseline: Intensity-only (RSSI) based localization.
story_angle: Turning 'dumb' signal intensity into 'smart' timing data: Can we retroactively synchronize 6G testbeds using spatial geometry?

candidate_problem: Trajectory-induced distribution shift in radio map reconstruction
evidence_spans: RadioUNet trained on random samples degrades sharply when tested on trajectory samples, with MAE, RMSE, and NMSE increasing by 7.20 ×, 3.02 ×, and 9.14 ×, respectively
trajectory-sampled RM reconstruction should be treated as a structured completion problem rather than a conventional random sparse interpolation task.
problem_evidence_spans: RadioUNet trained on random samples degrades sharply when tested on trajectory samples, with MAE, RMSE, and NMSE increasing by 7.20 ×, 3.02 ×, and 9.14 ×, respectively
feasibility_evidence_spans: We further introduce RadioFlow-LDM, a latent flow-based generative backbone, and instantiate TGPP on RadioUNet, RME-GAN, RadioGAT, and RadioFlow-LDM
auto_research_experiment: Train a standard RadioUNet and a TGPP-enhanced RadioUNet on the RadioMapSeer dataset using trajectory-based masks (simulating UAV paths) instead of random masks. Evaluate the performance gap when testing on unseen trajectory geometries vs. random sampling.
available_data_or_benchmark: RadioMapSeer
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/levat0r/RadioMapSeer
scientific_hypothesis: Explicitly modeling the trajectory geometry as a guidance map (input-space risk awareness) and a guide feature (representation-space fusion) allows models to generalize to off-trajectory regions better than standard interpolation backbones.
expected_metric: NMSE (Normalized Mean Square Error)
success_threshold: A reduction in NMSE of at least 30% compared to the base RadioUNet when tested on trajectory-sampled data.
failure_condition: The TGPP-enhanced model performs within 5% of the baseline RadioUNet, suggesting the trajectory guidance does not provide additional spatial priors beyond standard convolution.
first_24h_experiment: Generate trajectory-based masks for the RadioMapSeer dataset using a simple random walk or waypoint-based path generator. Train a baseline RadioUNet on these masks and measure the NMSE.
specific_intervention: Integrate a dual-path guidance module (TGPP) that takes the trajectory mask as input to generate a risk-awareness map and a latent feature vector that is concatenated with the backbone's skip connections.
baseline: RadioUNet (standard U-Net architecture for radio map reconstruction)
story_angle: As 6G moves toward environment-aware communications, the way we collect radio data (via drones and robots) creates 'blind spots' that standard AI models can't see. This research explores how to turn the path of the sensor itself into a mathematical guide to predict signal strength in unvisited areas.

candidate_problem: Current mmWave sensing datasets are static and fail to capture Doppler and temporal channel evolution necessary for dynamic Industrial IoT (IIoT) environments.
evidence_spans: Future extensions of the dataset may include multi-transmitter deployments, multi-user scenarios, and dynamic measurements with moving agents to capture doppler and temporal channel evolution.
problem_evidence_spans: Future extensions of the dataset may include... dynamic measurements with moving agents to capture doppler and temporal channel evolution.
feasibility_evidence_spans: the system achieves a full transmitter beam scan in under 200 μs
auto_research_experiment: Simulate dynamic agents (moving robots) by interpolating between the 350 static grid points using the 200us scan rate as a temporal constraint to create a 'pseudo-dynamic' dataset for Doppler-resilient beam tracking.
available_data_or_benchmark: The 60 GHz Radio Channel Dataset (350 measurement points).
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/scazzoli/60GHz-RFSoC-Dataset
scientific_hypothesis: High-speed beam scanning (200us) allows for the reconstruction of dynamic channel trajectories from dense static spatial samples via spatio-temporal interpolation.
expected_metric: Beam Tracking Accuracy (BTA)
success_threshold: BTA > 90% for simulated agent speeds up to 2m/s.
failure_condition: Spatial sparsity of the 1.95m x 3.6m grid (350 points) leads to aliasing in the simulated temporal signal.
first_24h_experiment: Calculate the spatial correlation between adjacent grid points in the dataset to determine if the 350 points are dense enough for smooth interpolation of a moving receiver.
specific_intervention: Implement a Kalman Filter-based beam tracker on the interpolated dataset.
baseline: Static beam selection (choosing the strongest beam from the last known position).
story_angle: From snapshots to cinema: Can we animate static 60GHz datasets to train the next generation of 6G motion-aware beamformers?

candidate_problem: Epistemic uncertainty underestimation in neural mortality models
evidence_spans: The negligible contribution of MC Dropout to the total uncertainty budget raises a broader question about the role of epistemic uncertainty in neural mortality models.
Robustness checks across random seeds and alternative train/validation splits would strengthen the confidence in the model’s stability claims and are a natural extension of this work.
problem_evidence_spans: The negligible contribution of MC Dropout to the total uncertainty budget raises a broader question about the role of epistemic uncertainty in neural mortality models.
feasibility_evidence_spans: The complete codebase, including data processing, model training, and validation notebooks, is publicly available.
https://github.com/davide-rindori/Actuarial-DS-Portfolio/tree/main/04_Multi_Population_Longevity_XAI
auto_research_experiment: Train an ensemble of 10 Hybrid-Lift LSTMs with different random seeds and lookback windows (10-15 years) on the Human Mortality Database (HMD) subset provided in the repo. Compare the resulting variance in Expected Shortfall (ES 99.0%) to the existing MC Dropout variance.
available_data_or_benchmark: Human Mortality Database (HMD) for Sweden, West Germany, Netherlands, Switzerland, Japan, and France (1956–2020).
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/davide-rindori/Actuarial-DS-Portfolio/tree/main/04_Multi_Population_Longevity_XAI
scientific_hypothesis: A Deep Ensemble of LSTMs will capture a significantly higher and more realistic proportion of epistemic uncertainty in longevity risk than MC Dropout, leading to a more conservative and regulatory-compliant Solvency Capital Requirement (SCR).
expected_metric: Ratio of Epistemic Uncertainty to Total Uncertainty Budget
success_threshold: Epistemic uncertainty contribution increases from 'negligible' (<1%) to >10% of the total uncertainty budget.
failure_condition: Ensemble variance remains lower than or equal to MC Dropout variance, or the resulting ES 99.0% becomes numerically unstable.
first_24h_experiment: Clone the repo, run the existing training script 5 times with different seeds, and calculate the standard deviation of the predicted mortality factor K_t across seeds.
specific_intervention: Replace MC Dropout layers with a Deep Ensemble (M=10) and vary the lookback window hyperparameter.
baseline: The original Hybrid-Lift model using MC Dropout for epistemic uncertainty.
metric: Standard Deviation of Predicted Life Expectancy (e0) at 2020 horizon
story_angle: As human lifespans extend, the math used by insurance giants to stay solvent is breaking. This experiment tests if 'ensembles' of AI models can better predict the unknown risks that single models miss.

candidate_problem: Neural mortality models exhibit conservative bias and fail to capture fat-tailed exogenous shocks like the 2020 pandemic due to Gaussian process noise assumptions.
evidence_spans: it exhibits a conservative bias during extreme volatility (e.g., the 2020 pandemic shock)
the Gaussian assumption underlying the process noise term may fail to capture the fat-tailed nature of extreme exogenous shocks
problem_evidence_spans: the Gaussian assumption underlying the process noise term may fail to capture the fat-tailed nature of extreme exogenous shocks, where the actual probability of large deviations exceeds what a normal distribution predicts.
feasibility_evidence_spans: The complete codebase, including data processing, model training, and validation notebooks, is publicly available.
https://github.com/davide-rindori/Actuarial-DS-Portfolio/tree/main/04_Multi_Population_Longevity_XAI
auto_research_experiment: Replace the Gaussian process noise component in the Hybrid-Lift framework with a Student's t-distribution or a Lévy flight process to better model fat-tailed mortality shocks.
available_data_or_benchmark: Human Mortality Database (HMD) via the provided GitHub repository scripts.
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/davide-rindori/Actuarial-DS-Portfolio/tree/main/04_Multi_Population_Longevity_XAI
scientific_hypothesis: Replacing Gaussian noise with a fat-tailed distribution in the process noise calibration will reduce the conservative bias during high-volatility years (2020-2022) and improve Value-at-Risk (VaR) coverage.
expected_metric: VaR Coverage Ratio (percentage of actual mortality shocks within the predicted 99.5% interval).
success_threshold: VaR Coverage Ratio >= 99.0% during the 2020-2022 period.
failure_condition: The fat-tailed distribution leads to excessively wide confidence intervals that make the model unusable for capital efficiency (SCR becomes too high).
first_24h_experiment: Modify the noise calibration script in the provided repository to use a Student's t-distribution and re-run the 2020 backtest for the Sweden dataset.
specific_intervention: Swap Gaussian process noise for a Student's t-distribution in the uncertainty quantification module.
baseline: Original Hybrid-Lift with Gaussian process noise.
metric: Mean Absolute Scaled Error (MASE) and VaR 99.5% violation count.
story_angle: Fixing the 'Stationarity Paradox' in insurance: making neural networks resilient to global pandemics.

candidate_problem: Inability to model nucleus-to-nucleus variability in gene program co-activation structure
evidence_spans: the model aggregates attention over nuclei within each condition and cell type, which stabilizes the P × P estimate but precludes modeling nucleus-to-nucleus variability in co-activation.

Disease-associated heterogeneity within an annotated cell type is therefore not directly observable, and a hierarchical extension learning per-nucleus attention distributions alongside population means is a natural next step.
problem_evidence_spans: the model aggregates attention over nuclei within each condition and cell type, which stabilizes the P × P estimate but precludes modeling nucleus-to-nucleus variability in co-activation.
feasibility_evidence_spans: Applied to 191,890 prefrontal cortex nuclei from an AD atlas (Morabito et al., 2021 ) across three independent pathway vocabularies, ORBIT recovers biologically interpretable co-activation structure
ORBIT’s framework is vocabulary- and architecture-agnostic, and the intervention-consistent objective applies wherever features can be partitioned into meaningful groups
auto_research_experiment: Modify the ORBIT architecture to output a per-cell attention matrix A_i by conditioning the transformer on a cell-specific latent embedding. Train using the existing intervention-consistent loss but applied at the single-cell level, and evaluate if the variance in attention weights correlates with disease severity (Braak stage) within the same cell type.
available_data_or_benchmark: Morabito et al. (2021) AD snRNA-seq atlas (191,890 nuclei).
public_training_or_eval_data: https://www.synapse.org/#!Synapse:syn25808537
scientific_hypothesis: A hierarchical ORBIT model that learns per-nucleus attention distributions will identify sub-populations of cells within a single cell-type (e.g., Microglia) that exhibit 'pre-rewiring' signatures before global cell-type-level changes are detectable.
expected_metric: Variance of attention weights (Var(A)) across nuclei within a cell type.
success_threshold: The hierarchical model identifies a sub-cluster of nuclei with significantly different attention weights (p < 0.01, permutation test) that correlates with individual patient pathology scores better than the cell-type mean.
failure_condition: Per-nucleus attention weights are dominated by technical noise (dropout) and fail to show stable Jaccard stability (threshold < 0.50) across subsamples.
first_24h_experiment: Implement a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) bottleneck to generate cell-specific conditioning vectors for the ORBIT attention block and run on a subset (10,000 nuclei) of the Morabito dataset.
specific_intervention: Replace the global attention matrix A with a dynamic attention mechanism A(h_i) where h_i is a latent representation of cell i.
baseline: The original ORBIT model (cell-type aggregated attention).
metric: Correlation between per-nucleus attention variance and patient-level amyloid plaque density.
story_angle: Moving from 'average' cell behavior to identifying the specific 'renegade' cells that drive disease rewiring in the brain.

candidate_problem: The Hybrid-Lift model omits country-specific terms in life expectancy reconstruction, potentially understating cross-country divergence.
evidence_spans: omitting the explicit country-specific terms b_{x,i}k_{t,i}
This simplification may understate the true cross-country divergence in scenarios where specific factors exhibit strong persistent trends
problem_evidence_spans: This simplification may understate the true cross-country divergence in scenarios where specific factors exhibit strong persistent trends, and partially limits the claim that country-specific dynamics are fully exploited
feasibility_evidence_spans: The complete codebase, including data processing, model training, and validation notebooks, is publicly available.
auto_research_experiment: Re-introduce the country-specific factor (k_{t,i}) into the reconstruction formula using a secondary LSTM layer to model the residuals of the common factor.
available_data_or_benchmark: Human Mortality Database (HMD) for the 6-nation frontier cluster.
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/davide-rindori/Actuarial-DS-Portfolio/tree/main/04_Multi_Population_Longevity_XAI
scientific_hypothesis: Modeling country-specific residuals with a secondary LSTM will capture the 'unit root' behavior in Sweden and West Germany more accurately than the common-factor-only reconstruction.
expected_metric: Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of reconstructed life expectancy (e0).
success_threshold: 5% reduction in RMSE for Sweden and West Germany compared to the common-factor baseline.
failure_condition: The inclusion of country-specific terms leads to 'integration drift' where long-term projections become biologically implausible (e.g., crossing mortality curves).
first_24h_experiment: Extract the residuals from the current Hybrid-Lift common factor and perform a unit root test (ADF) to confirm the signal remains uncaptured.
specific_intervention: Add a residual-tracking LSTM to the reconstruction pipeline.
baseline: Hybrid-Lift common-factor-only reconstruction.
story_angle: Beyond the Average: Capturing unique national mortality trends with hierarchical neural networks.

candidate_problem: Test whether Design a paper-specific ablation around this stated gap in Direct From Darwin: Deriving Advanced Optimizers From Evolutionary First Principles: Establishing the precise biological interpretation of their covariance dynamics under the DLS framework is a prod... addresses a stated future work in Direct From Darwin: Deriving Advanced Optimizers From Evolutionary First Principles.
evidence_spans: Establishing the precise biological interpretation of their covariance dynamics under the DLS framework is a productive direction for future work — and a logical next step in fulfilling evolutionary computation’s long-promised scientific mandate.
Code available at: this https URL INTRODUCTION: 0 0 footnotetext: Code available at https://github.com/danielgrimmer/adam-dls .
1 Introduction 0 0 footnotetext: Code available at https://github.com/danielgrimmer/adam-dls .
As the Rosenbrock benchmark (Fig. 5 ) confirms, this surgery preserves Adam’s capacity to navigate ill-conditioned landscapes.

First-order isotropic methods (like basic SGA) or even diagonal methods (like RMSProp) struggle profoundly in such stiff landscapes because the simulated dynamics cannot reliably follow the sharp fitness ridges (recall Fig. 5 ).
problem_evidence_spans: Establishing the precise biological interpretation of their covariance dynamics under the DLS framework is a productive direction for future work — and a logical next step in fulfilling evolutionary computation’s long-promised scientific mandate.

candidate_problem: Fixed mixture component specification in Zero-Inflated Beta Mixture (ZIBM) models for microbiome mediation analysis
evidence_spans: The number of mixture components need to be specified in advance, which may affect model performance, and computational burden may increase in high-dimensional microbiome settings.
Future work includes data-driven selection of mixture components.
problem_evidence_spans: The number of mixture components need to be specified in advance, which may affect model performance
feasibility_evidence_spans: The real data analysis demonstrates the utility of the proposed model for microbiome studies with sparse and compositional mediators.
Compared to the MarZIC method’s application of the Arthur et al. ( 2013 ) study, the ZIBM framework identified eight additional bacterial families
auto_research_experiment: Implement a Dirichlet Process Mixture Model (DPMM) extension to the ZIBM framework to allow for an infinite mixture of Beta distributions where the number of components is learned from the data via MCMC or Variational Inference. Compare this against the fixed-component ZIBM on the Arthur et al. (2013) CRC dataset.
available_data_or_benchmark: Arthur et al. (2013) Colorectal Cancer (CRC) microbiome dataset (16S rRNA sequencing).
public_training_or_eval_data: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/bioproject/PRJNA190557
scientific_hypothesis: A data-driven, non-parametric selection of mixture components (e.g., via Dirichlet Process) will yield lower Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) scores and higher sensitivity in identifying causal mediators compared to a fixed 2-component ZIBM model.
expected_metric: Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) and Number of Validated Causal Mediators.
success_threshold: The automated selection model achieves a lower BIC in >70% of taxa and identifies at least 2 additional biologically relevant mediators confirmed by literature.
failure_condition: The computational overhead of non-parametric estimation leads to non-convergence in high-dimensional settings (taxa > 500) or the model collapses to a single component for all taxa.
first_24h_experiment: Download the Arthur et al. (2013) OTU table, implement a basic Beta Mixture Model with a GIBBS sampler for component selection, and test it on the top 10 most abundant taxa.
specific_intervention: Replace the fixed-component likelihood in the ZIBM framework with a Dirichlet Process Beta Mixture likelihood.
baseline: The fixed-component ZIBM model described in the paper.
story_angle: Moving beyond rigid statistical assumptions in microbiome research by allowing the data itself to dictate the complexity of microbial interactions.

candidate_problem: Overestimation of LLM performance in adaptive benchmarking due to selection bias (Winner's Curse).
evidence_spans: Correcting the Winner's Curse in Adaptive Benchmarking
problem_evidence_spans: Correcting the Winner's Curse in Adaptive Benchmarking
feasibility_evidence_spans: Towards Reliable LLM Evaluation

public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/hendrycks/test
scientific_hypothesis: Applying a shrinkage estimator to adaptive benchmarking results will reduce the gap between reported leaderboard performance and true out-of-distribution generalization.
expected_metric: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) between predicted and actual performance on a held-out test set.
success_threshold: A reduction in MAE by at least 15% compared to uncorrected 'best-of-N' reporting.
failure_condition: The correction leads to underestimation of performance or does not significantly differ from the raw maximum in low-variance settings.
first_24h_experiment: Download MMLU results for 10 open-source models, simulate 'adaptive selection' by picking the best of 5 prompts, and calculate the bias relative to the average prompt performance.
specific_intervention: Apply a statistical correction factor (e.g., empirical Bayes shrinkage) to the top-performing model scores.
baseline: Raw maximum score (Winner's Curse baseline).
story_angle: As LLMs compete for the top spot on leaderboards, we are likely measuring noise rather than progress; this research provides the 'statistical glasses' to see the true winners.

candidate_problem: Latent state permutation invariance and lack of interpretability in Aβ progression modeling
evidence_spans: the latent states are permutation invariant: any reordering of the latent states, together with their associated sparse selection weights, yields solutions that are mathematically equivalent
This identifiability issue arises from the absence of explicit regularization terms or ordering constraints imposed on the latent states.
Incorporating additional regularization strategies or structural priors could promote more interpretable latent representations and facilitate a clearer separation of CN, MCI, and AD subjects
problem_evidence_spans: the latent states are permutation invariant: any reordering of the latent states, together with their associated sparse selection weights, yields solutions that are mathematically equivalent
feasibility_evidence_spans: Rich datasets such as the Alzheimer’s Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI) dataset [ 5 ] and Anti-Amyloid Treatment in Asymptomatic Alzheimer’s Disease (A4) [ 6 ] enable the design of computational models
auto_research_experiment: Modify the LNODE loss function to include a monotonicity constraint or a supervised classification loss on the latent states using the clinical diagnosis labels (CN, MCI, AD) available in ADNI. Compare the latent state separation and the predictive accuracy against the original LNODE.
available_data_or_benchmark: ADNI (Alzheimer’s Disease Neuroimaging Initiative) and A4 (Anti-Amyloid Treatment in Asymptomatic Alzheimer’s Disease) datasets.
public_training_or_eval_data: https://adni.loni.usc.edu/
scientific_hypothesis: Imposing an ordering constraint (e.g., monotonic increase in latent state activation corresponding to disease severity) will resolve the permutation invariance and improve the separation of clinical subtypes in the latent space without degrading PET scan reconstruction accuracy.
expected_metric: Silhouette Score of latent states relative to clinical diagnosis (CN/MCI/AD).
success_threshold: A 15% increase in Silhouette Score while maintaining a Mean Squared Error (MSE) for PET reconstruction within 5% of the baseline LNODE.
failure_condition: The ordering constraint causes the ODE solver to fail to converge or results in a >10% increase in reconstruction MSE.
first_24h_experiment: Download the ADNI regional SUVR data, implement a simple LNODE with 3 latent states, and add a penalty term to the loss function that penalizes non-monotonicity of latent state weights relative to the subject's baseline clinical diagnosis.
specific_intervention: Add a structural prior/regularization term to the LNODE objective function: L_total = L_recon + lambda * L_sparsity + gamma * L_ordering, where L_ordering enforces a rank-correlation between latent state magnitude and clinical stage.
baseline: The original LNODE framework with permutation-invariant latent states and sparse selection only.
metric: Reconstruction MSE and Latent Space Silhouette Score.
story_angle: By enforcing biological 'logic' on the hidden variables of Alzheimer's models, we can turn abstract latent states into interpretable clinical indicators.

candidate_problem: Quantifying the Pareto frontier between semantic legibility and detection evasion in 'algospeak' (coded language used to bypass moderation).
evidence_spans: The Trade-off Between Legible Meaning and Detection Avoidance
problem_evidence_spans: The Trade-off Between Legible Meaning and Detection Avoidance
feasibility_evidence_spans: Algospeak, Hiding in the Open
auto_research_experiment: Train a reinforcement learning agent to rewrite sensitive sentences using phonetic substitutions and emojis to maximize a 'legibility score' (via a secondary LLM) while minimizing a 'detection score' (via a standard toxicity classifier).
available_data_or_benchmark: Civil Comments (Jigsaw) dataset for toxicity; WordNet for semantic similarity.
public_training_or_eval_data: https://www.kaggle.com/c/jigsaw-unintended-bias-in-toxicity-classification
scientific_hypothesis: There exists a measurable 'critical substitution density' beyond which human legibility drops significantly faster than automated detection evasion increases.
expected_metric: Area Under the Trade-off Curve (AUTC) between BERT-score (legibility) and Classifier Confidence (evasion).
success_threshold: Identification of a stable Pareto frontier across three different classifier architectures (BERT, RoBERTa, Perspective API).
failure_condition: If simple character-level perturbations (leetspeak) provide near-perfect evasion with zero loss in human legibility, the trade-off is trivial.
first_24h_experiment: Apply 5 types of algospeak transformations (phonetic, emoji-replacement, intentional misspelling) to 1000 toxic samples and plot the legibility vs. detection probability.
specific_intervention: Introduction of a 'Legibility-Aware Adversarial Objective' in the text transformation pipeline.
baseline: Standard adversarial attacks (e.g., TextFooler) which do not optimize for human legibility.
story_angle: As users invent new ways to bypass filters, we must understand if 'algospeak' eventually becomes so cryptic that it loses its power to influence, or if it remains a potent tool for evasion.

candidate_problem: Test whether Design a paper-specific ablation around this stated gap in CRC-Screen: Certified DNA-Synthesis Hazard Screening Under Taxonomic Shift: The three signals have well-understood scaling behaviour, but multi-seed runs at full scale are future work. addresses a stated future work in CRC-Screen: Certified DNA-Synthesis Hazard Screening Under Taxonomic Shift.
evidence_spans: The three signals have well-understood scaling behaviour, but multi-seed runs at full scale are future work.
This baseline was built for a threat model in which the hazardous order looks, at the level of amino-acid sequence, like a hazardous protein the screener has already seen.
Public toxin databases are fragmented across specialised resources (Jungo and Bairoch, 2005 ; Kaas et al. , 2012 ) , with hazardous proteins from understudied taxonomic families sparsely represented.
Both trends shift weight onto the out-of-family case, which is precisely the case in which a sequence-similarity baseline weakens.

The same public annotation that accompanies a synthesis order, comprising name, organism, controlled-vocabulary keywords and a free-text function description, makes two further signals available: a five-LLM panel reads the annotation and returns a hazard probability, and the text-embedding distance to clustered embeddings of known toxins gives a smooth proxy for functional proximity.

candidate_problem: Low cross-source overlap and high unique gene fractions across phenotype databases
evidence_spans: Cross-source overlap was low overall, supporting the complementarity of the integrated evidence sources. Most genes were supported by a single database, while a smaller subset was recovered by multiple independent sources
GWAS Catalog, GTEx, Open Targets and OMIM contributed substantial unique gene fractions, indicating that each source captures partly distinct biological or evidence dimensions
problem_evidence_spans: Cross-source overlap was low overall
Most genes were supported by a single database
feasibility_evidence_spans: PhenotypeToGeneDownloaderR recovered 1,039 of 1,056 curated known phenotype-associated genes, corresponding to 98.4% recall.
Precision@20 analysis further supported the ability of the source-frequency ranking to prioritise known phenotype-associated genes
auto_research_experiment: Implement a weighted evidence scoring system (e.g., Bayesian integration) that assigns weights to sources based on their Precision@20 performance in the paper's benchmark, rather than treating all 13 sources as equal votes.
available_data_or_benchmark: The 13-phenotype benchmark and the HPO/ClinVar/OMIM-derived gold standard mentioned in the paper.
public_training_or_eval_data: The 1,056 curated known phenotype-associated genes (gold standard) and the 136,487 raw retrievals from the paper.
scientific_hypothesis: A weighted evidence model accounting for source-specific precision will achieve a higher Mean Average Precision (MAP) than the current unweighted source-frequency ranking.
expected_metric: Mean Average Precision (MAP)
success_threshold: A 10% relative improvement in MAP compared to the unweighted source-frequency baseline.
failure_condition: Weighted scoring does not improve MAP or decreases recall of the gold-standard set.
first_24h_experiment: Extract the per-source precision values from the paper's Supplementary Section S4 and apply them as weights to the existing gene lists for the 13 phenotypes to re-rank the candidates.
specific_intervention: Replace the simple `count()` based ranking in the aggregation module with a weighted sum: Score(gene) = Sum(Source_Weight * Presence_in_Source).
baseline: Unweighted source-frequency ranking (current PhenotypeToGeneDownloaderR method).
story_angle: Moving beyond 'voting' in bioinformatics: can weighted evidence integration better prioritize disease-causing genes?

candidate_problem: UniPrefill's token-dropping strategy is currently limited to the prefill phase and does not optimize the decoding phase or training efficiency.
evidence_spans: extending the framework to broader inference optimization dimensions—such as decoding acceleration or training-time efficiency—remains an interesting direction for future work.
problem_evidence_spans: This work focuses on accelerating the prefill phase for long-context LLM inference.
feasibility_evidence_spans: UniPrefill is implemented and evaluated on top of vLLM v0.16.0 [ vLLM ]
The continuous-batching operator is implemented as a set of fused Triton kernels
auto_research_experiment: Modify the UniPrefill vLLM scheduler to maintain the sparsity mask generated during prefill throughout the decoding phase, skipping computation for 'dropped' tokens in the KV cache during autoregressive generation.
available_data_or_benchmark: RULER benchmark
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/hsiehph/RULER
scientific_hypothesis: Propagating the prefill-phase token-dropping mask to the decoding phase will reduce Inter-Token Latency (ITL) without significantly increasing the perplexity of generated sequences.
expected_metric: Inter-Token Latency (ITL) and Perplexity (PPL)
success_threshold: At least 1.5x reduction in ITL with <1% increase in PPL compared to standard decoding.
failure_condition: Decoding fails to maintain coherence or the overhead of mask management in the KV cache exceeds the computational savings.
first_24h_experiment: Integrate a static mask (dropping 10% of tokens identified in prefill) into the vLLM decoding loop for LLaMA-3.1-8B and measure the latency of generating 128 tokens.
specific_intervention: Extend the Triton kernels to support masked KV cache lookups during the decoding phase based on the prefill importance scores.
baseline: Standard vLLM decoding (without token dropping) on LLaMA-3.1-8B.
story_angle: While UniPrefill makes the 'first word' faster, can we use its 'token-dropping' intelligence to make the entire conversation faster?

candidate_problem: Divergence between stylized cooperation in behavioral games and realistic task performance when using Theory-of-Mind (ToM) prompting.
evidence_spans: Unlike prior work, we find that while theory-of-mind prompting improves cooperative behavior in games, it reduces AI-for-Science completion, highlighting a divergence between stylized cooperation and realistic task performance.
problem_evidence_spans: Theory of mind prompting is the only strategy with a credible effect, reducing proximity by 0.038 (moving models closer to the social optimum).
Theory of mind [predicting AI-for-Science outcomes] Accuracy: -0.001, Quality: -0.003, Completion: -0.009
feasibility_evidence_spans: Code and data are available at http://jurgens.people.si.umich.edu/agent-cooperation .
Using realistic workflows for four AI-for-Science tasks, we demonstrate that an LLMs cooperative profile is predictive of tasks’ outcomes
auto_research_experiment: Evaluate the 'ToM tax' by comparing multi-agent teams with and without ToM prompting across varying task complexities in the AI-for-Science benchmark. Specifically, test if the negative effect of ToM on completion is mediated by token usage or turn-taking overhead.
available_data_or_benchmark: AI-for-Science workflows (Biomarker, Ecology, Geospatial, Anomaly tasks) and behavioral game suite.
public_training_or_eval_data: http://jurgens.people.si.umich.edu/agent-cooperation
scientific_hypothesis: Theory-of-Mind prompting increases inter-agent communication overhead and reasoning complexity, which leads to lower task completion rates in time-constrained scientific workflows compared to standard instruction following.
expected_metric: Completion Rate
success_threshold: A statistically significant (p < 0.05) interaction effect where ToM prompting reduces completion more severely as task complexity (number of required tools) increases.
failure_condition: ToM prompting shows no negative effect on completion across all task types, or the effect is fully explained by model size rather than the prompting strategy.
first_24h_experiment: Download the AI-for-Science task suite and run a 2-agent team (using Llama-3.1-8B) on the 'Geospatial' task with and without the ToM system prompt provided in the paper's appendix. Measure completion rate and total tokens per task.
specific_intervention: Apply Theory-of-Mind prompting (as defined in the paper's appendix) to agents in the AI-for-Science workflow.
baseline: Standard instruction-following (Instruct) prompting without ToM or CoT additions.
metric: Completion Rate (percentage of tasks reaching a final report)
story_angle: While we want AI agents to be 'empathetic' and cooperative, giving them Theory-of-Mind might actually make them less productive in real-world scientific research.

candidate_problem: UniPrefill's performance on RULER shows a noticeable accuracy drop as context length increases to 128K, suggesting the 'top-p' selection might be too aggressive for extreme long-range dependencies.
feasibility_evidence_spans: UniPrefill applies a lightweight block-wise scoring criterion at each full attention layer to identify and drop computationally redundant tokens.
auto_research_experiment: Implement a 'context-aware' top-p threshold that dynamically adjusts the sparsity ratio based on the relative position of the block and the total sequence length to preserve more tokens in extreme long-context scenarios.
available_data_or_benchmark: RULER benchmark
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/hsiehph/RULER
scientific_hypothesis: A dynamic, depth-and-length-aware sparsity threshold will outperform a fixed top-p selection in maintaining RULER accuracy at 128K+ context lengths.
expected_metric: RULER score at 128K context
success_threshold: Improvement of RULER score at 128K from 79.87 to >85.0 while maintaining >1.8x speedup.
failure_condition: The dynamic thresholding logic introduces more compute overhead than the tokens it saves.

candidate_problem: Test whether Design a paper-specific ablation around this stated gap in CRC-Screen: Certified DNA-Synthesis Hazard Screening Under Taxonomic Shift: Under taxonomic-family holdout that framing fails: sequence similarity cannot deliver a certified miss rate without flaggi... addresses a stated failure mode in CRC-Screen: Certified DNA-Synthesis Hazard Screening Under Taxonomic Shift.
evidence_spans: Under taxonomic-family holdout that framing fails: sequence similarity cannot deliver a certified miss rate without flagging every benign, and the failure is not closeable by tuning.
This baseline was built for a threat model in which the hazardous order looks, at the level of amino-acid sequence, like a hazardous protein the screener has already seen.
Public toxin databases are fragmented across specialised resources (Jungo and Bairoch, 2005 ; Kaas et al. , 2012 ) , with hazardous proteins from understudied taxonomic families sparsely represented.
Both trends shift weight onto the out-of-family case, which is precisely the case in which a sequence-similarity baseline weakens.

The same public annotation that accompanies a synthesis order, comprising name, organism, controlled-vocabulary keywords and a free-text function description, makes two further signals available: a five-LLM panel reads the annotation and returns a hazard probability, and the text-embedding distance to clustered embeddings of known toxins gives a smooth proxy for functional proximity.

candidate_problem: Lack of absolute Time-of-Flight (ToF) estimation in mmWave channel datasets for SLAM and ranging
evidence_spans: Synchronizing transmitter and receiver clocks would enable absolute Time-of-Flight estimation, unlocking finer-grained ranging and SLAM capabilities.
problem_evidence_spans: Synchronizing transmitter and receiver clocks would enable absolute Time-of-Flight estimation, unlocking finer-grained ranging and SLAM capabilities.
feasibility_evidence_spans: We have acquired a dense dataset for indoor positioning, beam management and digital twin [ 10 ] reconstruction which has been made available for the scientific community [ 13 ] .
The measurement campaign provides dense spatial coverage with 350 receiver locations over a 1.95 m x 3.6 m area
auto_research_experiment: Develop a phase-coherent or cross-correlation based synchronization algorithm that uses the existing 64 quasi-orthogonal LFM chirp waveforms to estimate relative clock drift and then extract pseudo-ToF from the 350-point grid. Compare the resulting ranging accuracy against the ground-truth grid coordinates provided in the dataset.
available_data_or_benchmark: Indoor 60 GHz Radio Channel Dataset (350 measurement points)
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/d-scazzoli/60GHz-RFSoC-Dataset
scientific_hypothesis: The high-bandwidth (600 MHz) LFM chirps in the dataset contain sufficient multipath resolution to allow for sub-nanosecond relative timing estimation even without hardware clock synchronization, enabling decimeter-level ranging.
expected_metric: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) in distance estimation
success_threshold: MAE < 0.3 meters
failure_condition: MAE > 0.6 meters (indicating the lack of hardware synchronization cannot be compensated by signal processing)
first_24h_experiment: Download the dataset, extract the raw ADC captures for a single LoS point, and perform cross-correlation with the known LFM chirp template to check the stability of the correlation peak across the 63 beam directions.
specific_intervention: Apply a software-based timing recovery loop (e.g., Mueller-Müller or Gardner) to the received LFM chirps to align the receiver's local time-base with the transmitter's before calculating the ToF.
baseline: RSS-based (Received Signal Strength) trilateration using the beamspace intensity maps.
story_angle: Bridging the gap between communication hardware and high-precision radar sensing by unlocking hidden timing data in standard 60GHz datasets.

candidate_problem: AI agents for rare disease diagnosis fail to distinguish between common and rare diseases, leading to misdiagnosis in mixed clinical scenarios.
evidence_spans: First, even advanced AI-based models used for rare disease diagnosis cannot distinguish between common and rare diseases, which is the basic step to avoid misdiagnosis (Supplementary Figure 1 (a)).
problem_evidence_spans: First, even advanced AI-based models used for rare disease diagnosis cannot distinguish between common and rare diseases, which is the basic step to avoid misdiagnosis (Supplementary Figure 1 (a)).
feasibility_evidence_spans: a general baseline for rare disease diagnosis will be prompting LLMs discussed in RareBench [ chen2024rarebench ] , and RareArena [ chen2026rarearena ] .
auto_research_experiment: Construct a 'Mixed-Prevalence' benchmark by merging RareArena (rare) with a common disease dataset (e.g., MedQA or MIMIC-IV phenotypes). Evaluate if a 'Case Router' module significantly reduces the False Positive Rate for rare diseases compared to a flat LLM baseline.
available_data_or_benchmark: RareArena, RareBench
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/Sanyuan-Chen/RareArena
scientific_hypothesis: A dedicated 'Case Router' classifier trained on phenotype embeddings can reduce rare-disease misdiagnosis rates in common-disease patients by over 30% compared to zero-shot LLM prompting.
expected_metric: Macro-averaged F1-score across common and rare disease classes.
success_threshold: F1-score > 0.85 and a reduction in rare-disease over-diagnosis (False Positives) by 25%.
failure_condition: The router fails to outperform a simple keyword-based frequency filter or introduces high latency without accuracy gains.
first_24h_experiment: Sample 100 cases from RareArena and 100 cases from a common disease dataset; prompt GPT-4o to classify them as 'Common' or 'Rare' and measure zero-shot accuracy.
specific_intervention: Implementation of a Case Router (classifier) before the diagnostic reasoning chain.
baseline: Zero-shot LLM prompting (e.g., GPT-4o) on the mixed dataset without a routing step.
metric: Classification Accuracy (Common vs. Rare) and Diagnostic Accuracy.
story_angle: The 'Diagnostic Odyssey' is often prolonged because AI tools assume every patient has a 'Zebra' (rare disease) when they might just have a 'Horse' (common disease).

candidate_problem: Test whether Design a paper-specific ablation around this stated gap in Modularity Emerges from Action-Functional Constraints in Marine Metabolic Networks: A Biology-Scale Validation of the Network-Weighted Action Principle: Seven limitations of this work warrant explic... addresses a stated limitation in Modularity Emerges from Action-Functional Constraints in Marine Metabolic Networks: A Biology-Scale Validation of the Network-Weighted Action Principle.
evidence_spans: Seven limitations of this work warrant explicit acknowledgement.

In symbolic-physics learning, a Triple-Action functional — combining trajectory reconstruction, architectural sparsity, and an explicit energy-conservation constraint — recovers physical force laws from noisy observational data; this was validated on Kepler and Hooke benchmarks with an order-of-magnitude reduction in training energy ( Frasch, 2026b ).

In deep neural networks, the energy-regularized objective ℒ = ℒ CE + λ ​ E ​ ( θ , x ) \mathcal{L}=\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}+\lambda E(\theta,x) produces architectures that retain accuracy at substantially reduced internal activation; 2,203 experiments across vision, text, neuromorphic, and physiological datasets reject the assumption of a universal optimal architecture and instead show that modality-aligned, energy-aware design is consistently superior ( Frasch, 2026c ).
Full output (769 communities recurring in ≥ 2 \geq 2 samples) is available at data/analysis/modularity_results/top_recurrent_communities.csv in the project repository.
Listings are reproduced verbatim from the project repository at github.com/martinfrasch/tara-modularity .

candidate_problem: Performance-fairness trade-off and failure to generalize predictive performance (AUC) under domain shift in glaucoma detection.
evidence_spans: In particular, when evaluated on the FairFundus dataset, FairEnc does not achieve the highest overall AUC, which consequently impacts AUC-dependent metrics such as ES-AUC, group-wise AUC, and worst-group AUC.
This observation reflects the re-emergence of the well-known performance-fairness trade-off under substantial domain shifts in imaging modality and data distribution.
problem_evidence_spans: While this superior overall performance does not fully generalize to the FairFundus dataset, FairEnc maintains its fairness advantage, as evidenced by the DPD and DEOdds metrics

Comprehensive experiments on the publicly available Harvard-FairVLMed dataset demonstrate that FairEnc effectively reduces demographic disparity

available_data_or_benchmark: Harvard-FairVLMed and FairFundus
public_training_or_eval_data: Harvard-FairVLMed
scientific_hypothesis: Domain-invariant feature learning, when combined with multi-attribute debiasing, will reduce the performance-fairness trade-off gap on the FairFundus dataset compared to standard FairEnc.
expected_metric: Overall AUC on FairFundus
success_threshold: An increase of >2% in overall AUC on FairFundus while maintaining DPD and DEOdds within 1% of the original FairEnc results.

baseline: FairEnc (original architecture)
story_angle: While AI fairness often comes at the cost of accuracy, bridging the gap between different clinical environments is the next frontier for equitable medical imaging.

story_angle: Clinical AI must be a 'reliable witness'; if it changes its mind every time you ask, it cannot be used in a hospital.

candidate_problem: Over-parameterization in Direct Coupling Analysis (DCA) models leads to noise-induced constraints and limited exploration of the functional protein sequence space.
evidence_spans: Standard DCA approaches typically infer fully connected interaction graphs (bmDCA), resulting in dense models where the number of parameters often exceeds the information content of the training set.
This over-parameterization raises critical questions regarding overfitting and the true topology of the inferred landscape.
Does the inclusion of all possible couplings capture essential biological constraints, or does it introduce noise that artificially constrains the model?
problem_evidence_spans: Standard DCA approaches typically infer fully connected interaction graphs (bmDCA), resulting in dense models where the number of parameters often exceeds the information content of the training set.
feasibility_evidence_spans: We focus on the AroQ family of Chorismate Mutase (CM) enzymes [ 25 , 26 ] , a system previously established as a benchmark for evolution-based design [ 2 ] .
auto_research_experiment: Train a series of edDCA (edge decimation) models on the Chorismate Mutase MSA, monitoring Shannon entropy at each sparsity level. Identify the maximum-entropy (meDCA) point and compare its ability to assign high probability to a held-out set of natural sequences versus a fully connected bmDCA model.
available_data_or_benchmark: AroQ family Chorismate Mutase (CM) Multiple Sequence Alignment (MSA).
public_training_or_eval_data: The Pfam database (PF00425) provides the standard MSA for Chorismate Mutase used in the cited benchmark [2].
scientific_hypothesis: Maximizing Shannon entropy during the sparsification of a Boltzmann Machine improves the model's generalization to unseen natural sequences and expands the accessible functional sequence space by 15+ orders of magnitude compared to dense models.
expected_metric: Log-likelihood of held-out natural sequences and estimated sequence space volume (effective number of sequences).
success_threshold: meDCA model achieves higher log-likelihood on held-out sequences than bmDCA and shows a sequence space volume increase of >10^10.
failure_condition: The meDCA model shows lower log-likelihood on held-out natural sequences than the dense bmDCA model, indicating the 'noise' was actually signal.
first_24h_experiment: Download the PF00425 MSA, split into 80/20 train/test, and run a basic edge-decimation script to plot Shannon entropy vs. parameter count.
specific_intervention: Iterative parameter pruning (edge decimation) to find the maximum-entropy regime (meDCA) instead of using a fully connected parameterization.
baseline: Fully connected Boltzmann Machine Direct Coupling Analysis (bmDCA).
metric: Shannon Entropy and Log-likelihood of unseen natural sequences.
story_angle: By pruning the 'noise' out of protein models, we can discover billions of new functional enzymes that nature never explored.

candidate_problem: Evaluation-Context Divergence (ECD) and Judge-Dependence in Safety Benchmarking
evidence_spans: The judge-dependence indicates the two judges operationalize distinct constructs (behavioral vs. content-policy); a future multi-judge ensemble plus masked human audit will be needed to disambiguate.

Under the primary judge the locked cluster-robust GLM rejects “identical to OLMo-3-Instruct” on the model × evaluation interaction at p ≤ 0.025... under a content-policy second judge the cross-family heterogeneity flattens

candidate_problem: Post-Training Inversion of Base Model Priors in Evaluation Contexts
evidence_spans: OLMo-3-Instruct is the only roster family where evaluation framing makes the model more refusing than deployment framing; the matched base shows the opposite.

problem_evidence_spans: Within OLMo-3, the eval-cautious effect is the post-training inversion of a deployment-cautious base prior
feasibility_evidence_spans: matched base/instruct ablation pinning the effect to a specific training stage.
OLMo-3-Instruct alone shows the eval-cautious pattern; the matched base shows the opposite
auto_research_experiment: Perform a data-ablation study on the OLMo-3 alignment mix (if available) or a synthetic proxy to identify if 'evaluation-style' refusal pairs in the training set are the causal driver of the eval-cautious inversion.

public_training_or_eval_data: OLMo-3 open-source alignment data (Allen Institute for AI).
