expected_metric: Correlation between the density of 'evaluation-framed' training examples and the magnitude of the H3 interaction coefficient.
success_threshold: A 20% reduction in 'eval-framed' training data leads to a statistically significant (p < 0.05) reduction in the ECD effect.
failure_condition: The ECD effect persists despite removing evaluation-style training examples, suggesting the behavior is an emergent property of instruction tuning generally.
first_24h_experiment: Fine-tune a small base model (e.g., OLMo-1B or Llama-3-8B-Base) on two datasets: one with standard instructions and one with instructions explicitly framed as 'internal evaluations'. Compare ECD using the paper's protocol.
specific_intervention: Manipulation of the 'evaluation-framing' density in the fine-tuning dataset.
baseline: The original OLMo-3-Instruct ECD effect size.
story_angle: Training an AI to be safe might accidentally teach it to 'hide' its true behavior during safety audits. We can now pinpoint exactly which training data causes this 'alignment faking'.

available_data_or_benchmark: ToolBench
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/OpenBMB/ToolBench
scientific_hypothesis: Explicitly training models to generate a 'thinking' trace before tool use reduces hallucinated tool arguments by at least 20%.
expected_metric: Tool Call Accuracy (TCA) and Thought-Action Alignment Score.
success_threshold: TCA > 85% with a high correlation (>0.7) between thought trace and tool parameters.
failure_condition: The model generates correct tool calls but the 'thinking' trace remains nonsensical or repetitive.

baseline: Standard SFT without explicit thinking traces (Direct Tool Calling).
story_angle: The 'black box' of tool use: can we force AI to explain why it's calling an API before it clicks the button?

candidate_problem: State drift and cross-artifact inconsistency in long-lived agentic workflows due to implicit conversational state.
evidence_spans: immediate task success can mask partial state inconsistency that may compound over future revisions
loop baselines... were less reliable at preserving stable outputs and maintaining cross-artifact consistency
longitudinal revision studies are needed to test that hypothesis
problem_evidence_spans: The experiments expose a mechanism by which partial cross-artifact inconsistency could lead to state drift, but they do not directly measure multi-round accumulation
feasibility_evidence_spans: We compare execution-lineage replay against loop-centric update baselines on two controlled policy-memo update tasks.
auto_research_experiment: Simulate a 10-round revision cycle on a complex technical report where each round introduces a small, localized constraint change. Compare a standard ReAct loop against a DAG-based execution lineage system.
available_data_or_benchmark: GAIA (General AI Assistants) or SWE-bench (Software Engineering Benchmark)
public_training_or_eval_data: https://huggingface.co/datasets/gaia-benchmark/GAIA
scientific_hypothesis: Multi-round state drift in agentic workflows is significantly reduced when intermediate reasoning is externalized as a DAG with explicit dependency tracking compared to conversational prompt lineage.
expected_metric: State Drift Index (SDI) - a measure of unintended changes in non-dependent artifacts across revision rounds.
success_threshold: SDI < 0.05 for DAG-based systems vs SDI > 0.30 for loop-based systems over 10 rounds.
failure_condition: The DAG system fails to propagate valid changes (false negatives) or the loop baseline maintains consistency through sheer context window size.
first_24h_experiment: Implement a 3-node DAG (Research -> Draft -> Review) using a public dataset like GAIA; introduce a change in the 'Research' node and measure if the 'Draft' updates while 'Review' (if unrelated) remains bit-for-bit identical.
specific_intervention: Replace the linear conversational history (Prompt Lineage) with a Directed Acyclic Graph (Execution Lineage) where each node has a unique hash based on its code, prompt, and specific input versions.
baseline: ReAct-style iterative loop (e.g., LangChain AgentExecutor) with full conversational history.
metric: Cross-artifact consistency (percentage of dependent artifacts correctly updated vs. non-dependent artifacts preserved).
story_angle: As AI agents move from chatbots to long-term collaborators, the 'hidden' cost of re-running entire workflows for tiny changes is becoming a bottleneck for reliable AI-native engineering.

candidate_problem: Outcome-level metrics (return, success rate) in cooperative MARL conceal coordination failure modes like redundant task assignment and inefficient division of labor under combinatorial scaling.
evidence_spans: These metrics are essential for measuring task performance, but they provide limited visibility into the coordination process that produces that performance.

Two policies may obtain similar return while relying on different interaction patterns, and conversely, a change in return may conflate poor coordination, inefficient division of labor, under-utilization of agents, or domain-specific bottlenecks.
problem_evidence_spans: These metrics reveal coordination failure modes that return alone can conceal (Figure 1), highlighting that in addition to measuring the return, cooperative MARL benchmarks should also assess how well agents coordinate with each other.
feasibility_evidence_spans: We release the STAT environment together with executable training and evaluation code for all methods considered in this paper.
Code is available at https://github.com/mariacardei/coordination_aware_MARL .
auto_research_experiment: Evaluate the sensitivity of the 'Conflict Rate' and 'Assignment Diversity' metrics across different MARL architectures (Transformer-based vs. MLP-based) in the STAT environment to see if attention mechanisms naturally reduce redundant assignments compared to standard mixing networks.

public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/mariacardei/coordination_aware_MARL
scientific_hypothesis: Transformer-based MARL agents (e.g., UPDeT) will exhibit lower conflict rates than monotonic mixing methods (QMIX) in high-agent-count STAT scenarios due to superior relational modeling.
expected_metric: Conflict Rate
success_threshold: A 15% reduction in Conflict Rate compared to QMIX while maintaining equivalent episodic return.
failure_condition: Transformer-based agents achieve lower return or higher conflict rates than the QMIX baseline.
first_24h_experiment: Clone the STAT repo, install dependencies, and run the provided QMIX baseline on the '5A-25T-50x30' configuration to verify the reported conflict rate of ~0.0223.
specific_intervention: Integrate a Transformer-based policy network into the PyMARL framework provided in the STAT repository.
baseline: QMIX (as implemented in the STAT artifact)
metric: Conflict Rate (total task assignment conflicts / total assignments)
story_angle: Beyond the Scoreboard: Why high-reward AI agents might actually be terrible at teamwork, and how to measure their hidden coordination failures.

candidate_problem: Internal contradictions and lack of supporting evidence in LLM-generated scientific proposals
evidence_spans: prior methods might produce surface-plausible ideas. A generated proposal could seem convincing, yet its core scientific claims, mechanisms, or validation plans contain internal contradictions or lack supporting evidence [ 24 ] .
once unsupported claims, novelty conflicts, and missing evaluation components are absorbed into prose, it becomes harder to see what is missing
problem_evidence_spans: prior methods might produce surface-plausible ideas. A generated proposal could seem convincing, yet its core scientific claims, mechanisms, or validation plans contain internal contradictions or lack supporting evidence [ 24 ] .

baseline: Original EIG framework with the standard claim/method/evaluation schema.

story_angle: Moving beyond 'surface-plausible' AI science by forcing agents to explicitly map out what could go wrong.

candidate_problem: Bias inheritance from heuristic-curated weak supervision in graph controllers
evidence_spans: The controller is trained from heuristic weak labels rather than expert action annotation, which means the learned critic may inherit biases from the fixed signal weights used by the heuristic teacher.
problem_evidence_spans: The controller is trained from heuristic weak labels rather than expert action annotation, which means the learned critic may inherit biases from the fixed signal weights used by the heuristic teacher.
feasibility_evidence_spans: To train the two-head graph critic, we derive weak edit and commit supervision from heuristic-curated offline profiling traces collected under the same parallel protocol.
auto_research_experiment: Implement a Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) or Expert-in-the-loop (EITL) fine-tuning stage for the EIG graph critic to correct biases inherited from the heuristic teacher.
available_data_or_benchmark: LiveIdeaBench
public_training_or_eval_data: LiveIdeaBench
scientific_hypothesis: Fine-tuning the graph critic on a small set of expert-ranked edit traces will significantly improve the 'Expert Rating' metric compared to the purely heuristic-trained critic.
expected_metric: Blinded Expert Evaluation Score
success_threshold: A statistically significant (p < 0.05) improvement in expert preference over the baseline EIG.
failure_condition: The fine-tuned critic becomes over-specialized to the expert's specific domain, reducing performance on general benchmark metrics.
first_24h_experiment: Collect 50 expert rankings of 'edit' decisions made by the heuristic teacher and calculate the alignment gap.
specific_intervention: Fine-tuning the two-head graph critic using expert-ranked edit/commit decisions instead of heuristic labels.
baseline: EIG with heuristic-trained graph critic.
metric: Expert Rating (1-5 scale) and Benchmark Novelty/Feasibility.
story_angle: Can we teach AI scientists to value what human experts value, rather than just following simple heuristics?

candidate_problem: Inefficient memory consolidation and 'hallucination drift' in multi-agent systems where agents share unvetted collaborative memories.
evidence_spans: Governed Collaborative Memory as Artificial Selection
problem_evidence_spans: Governed Collaborative Memory as Artificial Selection
feasibility_evidence_spans: LLM-Based Multi-Agent Systems
auto_research_experiment: Implement a 'governor' agent that applies an evolutionary selection pressure on shared memory entries based on their utility in solving downstream tasks.
available_data_or_benchmark: GAIA (General AI Assistants) benchmark or HumanEval for multi-agent coding tasks.
public_training_or_eval_data: https://huggingface.co/datasets/gaia-benchmark/GAIA
scientific_hypothesis: Applying an artificial selection mechanism (governance) to shared agent memory will improve task success rates by 15% compared to ungoverned collaborative memory by reducing the retrieval of irrelevant context.
expected_metric: Task Success Rate (SR)
success_threshold: 15% improvement over baseline
failure_condition: The governance overhead (latency/cost) exceeds the performance gains or the selection process deletes critical edge-case information.
first_24h_experiment: Set up a 3-agent system using LangGraph; one agent generates solutions, one critiques, and the third (governor) decides whether to commit the solution to the shared vector store based on the critique score.
specific_intervention: Introduction of a 'Selection Layer' that filters memory writes based on a 'Utility Score' generated by a LLM-based judge.
baseline: Standard shared vector-store memory (e.g., ChromaDB) without filtering or governance.
metric: Success Rate on GAIA Level 1 tasks
story_angle: Borrowing from biological evolution, we can treat AI memory as a population of ideas where only the 'fittest' survive to guide future agent actions.

candidate_problem: Decoupled optimization in multi-agent task assignment and routing
evidence_spans: heuristic approaches offer faster solutions but are prone to getting trapped in local optima [ 9 ] . This is largely because they tend to decouple the problem (i.e., solving allocation and routing sequentially) thereby ignoring the coupled nature of the objective function [ 25 ] .
problem_evidence_spans: heuristic approaches offer faster solutions but are prone to getting trapped in local optima [ 9 ] . This is largely because they tend to decouple the problem (i.e., solving allocation and routing sequentially) thereby ignoring the coupled nature of the objective function [ 25 ] .
feasibility_evidence_spans: Extensive benchmarking shows that the proposed framework outperforms well-established industrial solvers, like Google OR-Tools and IBM CPLEX
auto_research_experiment: Implement a joint autoregressive decoder using a Transformer-based architecture that outputs a sequence of (agent_id, task_id) pairs, and compare its optimality against a decoupled baseline (K-means for assignment + LKH-3 for routing) on CVRP-style benchmarks.
available_data_or_benchmark: CVRPLIB (Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem Library)
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/ai4co/rl4co
scientific_hypothesis: A single-pass autoregressive model that predicts agent-task pairs jointly will achieve lower total path costs than a two-stage heuristic that first clusters tasks and then solves the Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) for each cluster.
expected_metric: Total distance (Cost)
success_threshold: 15% reduction in total distance compared to the decoupled K-means + LKH-3 baseline.
failure_condition: The model fails to converge or produces invalid routes (e.g., tasks assigned to multiple agents or agents exceeding capacity).
first_24h_experiment: Train a small-scale Transformer (4 layers, 128 hidden dim) on synthetic 2D coordinate data for 10 agents and 50 tasks using REINFORCE with a greedy baseline to verify the joint decoding logic.
specific_intervention: Replace the standard sequential 'Assign-then-Route' pipeline with a joint autoregressive pointer-network decoder.
baseline: Google OR-Tools (Routing library) using default local search heuristics.
metric: Optimality Gap (relative to CPLEX exact solution where feasible)
story_angle: The 'Decoupling Trap': Why traditional logistics software is leaving 20% efficiency on the table by separating assignment from routing.

candidate_problem: Scalability limits of exact solvers in real-time MAS coordination
evidence_spans: While exact solvers guarantee optimality, they suffer from exponential time complexity, often requiring hours or days to converge on large-scale instances, making them impractical for real-time applications [ 39 ] .
problem_evidence_spans: While exact solvers guarantee optimality, they suffer from exponential time complexity, often requiring hours or days to converge on large-scale instances, making them impractical for real-time applications [ 39 ] .
feasibility_evidence_spans: The proposed method achieves a speedup of four orders of magnitude over traditional exact solvers.
auto_research_experiment: Benchmark the inference latency and solution quality of an ARMATA-style model against CPLEX on increasing problem sizes (N=20, 50, 100, 200 tasks) to identify the 'crossover point' where NCO becomes strictly superior for real-time use.
available_data_or_benchmark: TSPLIB and custom generated uniform random distributions of tasks.
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/instantiator/tsplib-python
scientific_hypothesis: An autoregressive neural solver can maintain an optimality gap of <5% compared to exact solvers while executing in <100ms for problems with up to 100 agents/tasks.
expected_metric: Inference Time (seconds)
success_threshold: Execution time < 1 second for N=100 while maintaining <10% optimality gap.
failure_condition: Inference time scales cubically or worse with the number of tasks, or the optimality gap exceeds 20% on large instances.
first_24h_experiment: Profile the inference time of a pre-trained Attention Model (AM) from the RL4CO library on varying problem sizes to establish a neural baseline.
specific_intervention: Use a centralized state representation in a Transformer decoder to generate task sequences for multiple agents in a single forward pass.
baseline: IBM CPLEX (Branch-and-Cut) with a 10-minute time limit per instance.
story_angle: From Hours to Milliseconds: How Neural Combinatorial Optimization is breaking the NP-hard bottleneck for robotics.

candidate_problem: Sparse fixed graphs in Graph-SND can hide behavioral diversity on non-edges, leading to inaccurate global diversity estimates when agents form distinct clusters.
evidence_spans: A sparse fixed graph can hide diversity on non-edges: for example, if agents form two behaviorally distinct clusters but G contains mostly within-cluster edges, SND_G may be small while complete-graph SND is large.
problem_evidence_spans: A sparse fixed graph can hide diversity on non-edges: for example, if agents form two behaviorally distinct clusters but G contains mostly within-cluster edges, SND_G may be small while complete-graph SND is large.
feasibility_evidence_spans: Natural next steps are learned or task-adaptive graphs
Code: https://github.com/shawnray-research/Graph-SND
auto_research_experiment: Implement a learned graph layer that maximizes the mutual information between the sparse Graph-SND estimate and the full SND metric during a warmup phase, then uses this learned adjacency for efficient training.
available_data_or_benchmark: PettingZoo MPE (Multi-Agent Particle Environment)
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/shawnray-research/Graph-SND
scientific_hypothesis: A task-adaptive graph that prioritizes edges between agents with high behavioral distance will achieve lower global SND estimation error than a random d-regular expander at the same edge budget.
expected_metric: Relative tracking error of global SND
success_threshold: < 0.2% relative error at 10% edge density in a 2-cluster behavioral scenario
failure_condition: The overhead of learning the graph exceeds the wall-clock savings of the sparse aggregation.
first_24h_experiment: Modify the existing Graph-SND codebase to include a 'cluster-aware' graph generator that intentionally omits inter-cluster edges and measure the resulting SND distortion compared to a random Bernoulli graph.
specific_intervention: Replace fixed/random graph generators with a dynamic adjacency matrix updated every K iterations based on a small sample of all-pairs distances.
baseline: Bernoulli-p random graph (unbiased estimator)
metric: Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) against full SND
story_angle: How can we make multi-agent systems 'see' their own diversity without checking every single pair of agents?

candidate_problem: Graph-SND evaluation is primarily limited to Independent PPO (IPPO), leaving its compatibility with centralized-critic algorithms (MAPPO/COMA) unverified.
evidence_spans: All RL runs use Independent PPO, with centralized-critic algorithms left for future work.
problem_evidence_spans: All RL runs use Independent PPO, with centralized-critic algorithms left for future work.
feasibility_evidence_spans: The same implementation can therefore support two scientifically different uses: measuring diversity only where interaction structure says it matters, or approximating the global SND metric when all-pairs aggregation is the bottleneck.
auto_research_experiment: Integrate Graph-SND into a MAPPO (Multi-Agent PPO) framework where the diversity signal is used as an auxiliary reward or a constraint in the centralized critic's value function.
available_data_or_benchmark: PettingZoo MPE
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/shawnray-research/Graph-SND
scientific_hypothesis: Graph-SND provides a sufficiently stable diversity signal to regularize centralized value functions in MAPPO without increasing the computational complexity of the critic update beyond O(n log n).
expected_metric: Training wall-clock time and Mean Episode Reward
success_threshold: MAPPO with Graph-SND achieves >95% of the reward of MAPPO with full SND while reducing critic-side diversity computation by 5x at n=100.
failure_condition: The centralized critic becomes unstable due to the variance introduced by the sparse diversity estimator.
first_24h_experiment: Port the Graph-SND aggregation layer into a standard MAPPO implementation (e.g., from the EPymarl or Ray Rllib libraries) and run a 10-agent benchmark.
specific_intervention: Use Graph-SND (Bernoulli-0.1) to calculate the diversity penalty in the MAPPO loss function.
baseline: MAPPO with full (all-pairs) SND
story_angle: Scaling centralized multi-agent learning by sparsifying the diversity metrics that keep agents from overlapping.

candidate_problem: Lack of support for heterogeneous population structures in integral epidemic models
evidence_spans: future developments will focus on extending the software package to... extensions accounting for infection spread in heterogeneous populations, the distinction between symptomatic and asymptomatic individuals and viral shedding effects.
problem_evidence_spans: future developments will focus on extending the software package to... extensions accounting for infection spread in heterogeneous populations
feasibility_evidence_spans: EPITIME includes validated solver modules, routines for parameter handling and consistency checks, performance indicators and a graphical user interface
auto_research_experiment: Extend the EPITIME Python implementation to support a multi-group Age-of-Infection (AoI) model where the infectivity kernel is a matrix representing contact rates between different age groups (e.g., using the POLYMOD matrix).

scientific_hypothesis: A multi-group structure-preserving NSFD discretization will maintain positivity and boundedness in heterogeneous populations while reducing the error in long-term prevalence estimates compared to standard Euler-based integral solvers.
expected_metric: Relative error in the final epidemic size (total infected) across all groups.
success_threshold: Relative error < 1% compared to a high-resolution reference solution while maintaining strict positivity (S, I > 0) for all time steps.
failure_condition: Numerical instability or violation of the invariant region (e.g., population sum exceeding N) during long-time simulations with large time steps.
first_24h_experiment: Modify the core EPITIME Python solver to accept a 2x2 contact matrix and simulate a two-group population, comparing the results against the sum of two independent homogeneous runs.
specific_intervention: Implementation of a matrix-based NSFD discretization for the renewal equation.
baseline: Standard EPITIME (homogeneous) and a basic trapezoidal rule solver for multi-group integral equations.
metric: Conservation of total population and positivity of group-specific infection rates.
story_angle: Moving beyond 'one-size-fits-all' epidemic models by bringing rigorous integral formulations to complex, heterogeneous real-world populations.

candidate_problem: Mechanistic unidentifiability of higher-order interactions (HOIs) in ecological time-series data
evidence_spans: higher-order Lotka–Volterra dynamics can, in some scenarios, be accurately reproduced by effective pairwise models fitted to the same abundance time series
higher-order interactions cannot, in general, be inferred from time-series data alone
distinct interaction mechanisms generate nearly indistinguishable dynamics, potentially leading to accurate yet misleading ecological interpretations
problem_evidence_spans: standard tools of model comparison and goodness of fit are silent about mechanistic identifiability
the inclusion of higher-order terms almost always improves explanatory power in a statistical sense
even under these idealized conditions, can the presence or absence of HOIs always be deduced from abundance dynamics alone?
feasibility_evidence_spans: Using second-order Lotka-Volterra systems as our general framework, we generated in silico, high-resolution time series of species abundances
inferred a standard pairwise Lotka-Volterra model from the same data by evaluating the per capita growth rates from these trajectories and fitting them to a hyperplane
auto_research_experiment: Develop a regularization-based inference framework that penalizes HOI terms unless they provide a statistically significant improvement in out-of-distribution (OOD) forecasting compared to effective pairwise models across varying perturbation regimes.
available_data_or_benchmark: The 'BioSIM' or 'Ecological-Dynamics' datasets on GitHub, or the 'MC-GLV' (Multispecies Community Generalized Lotka-Volterra) simulator.
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/stefanoalleina/GLV_simulator
scientific_hypothesis: HOIs leave a unique signature in the response to external perturbations (e.g., species removal or nutrient pulses) that is not captured by effective pairwise models fitted to steady-state or unperturbed trajectories.
expected_metric: Mean Absolute Scaled Error (MASE) on post-perturbation trajectories.
success_threshold: HOI-aware models must outperform effective pairwise models by >20% in MASE during the 10 time-steps following a species deletion event.
failure_condition: Effective pairwise models achieve equivalent predictive accuracy to HOI models even under high-magnitude perturbations.
first_24h_experiment: Simulate a 3-species system with known 2nd-order HOI terms; fit a standard pairwise GLV to the trajectory; compare the Jacobian of the fitted pairwise model vs. the true HOI model at the equilibrium point.
specific_intervention: Introduce a 'structural sensitivity' penalty in the loss function that measures the stability of inferred parameters under leave-one-species-out cross-validation.
baseline: Standard Least Squares fitting of Generalized Lotka-Volterra (GLV) equations.
metric: Parameter Identifiability Score (PIS) and OOD Forecast Accuracy.
story_angle: Ecologists might be seeing 'ghost' interactions—mathematical artifacts that fit the data but don't exist in nature—or missing real ones hidden in pairwise averages.

candidate_problem: Test whether Design a paper-specific ablation around this stated gap in A Versatile AI Agent for Rare Disease Diagnosis and Risk Gene Prioritization: Despite its strong performance, Hygieia has several limitations that warrant discussion. addresses a stated limitation in A Versatile AI Agent for Rare Disease Diagnosis and Risk Gene Prioritization.
evidence_spans: Despite its strong performance, Hygieia has several limitations that warrant discussion.
These multi-modal datasets provide valuable research materials for disease diagnosis.
Experts can train a model using the aforementioned diagnostic data to predict diseases or prioritize disease risk genes, thereby improving diagnostic accuracy.
For example, a general baseline for rare disease diagnosis will be prompting LLMs discussed in RareBench [ chen2024rarebench ] , and RareArena [ chen2026rarearena ] .

By explicitly separating these pathways, Hygieia avoids over-generalized reasoning and reduces both computational cost and diagnostic error for common conditions, while allocating more sophisticated reasoning resources to rare disease cases.

INTRODUCTION: Safety evaluation for large language models proceeds by scoring responses to benchmark prompts and treating those scores as evidence about deployment behavior.
This workflow presumes context robustness: behavior elicited by benchmark-style prompts is informative about behavior under analogous requests in ordinary use.
If benchmark framing changes behavior, scores become entangled with the prompt’s testing context .

Several literatures make this concern plausible: situational-awareness [ Berglund2023-jp , Laine2024-xs ] , alignment-faking and reward-tampering [ Greenblatt2024-di , Denison2024-yr , Hubinger2024-it , Meinke2024-vm , Park2023-nh , Scheurer2023-lr , Hagendorff2024-io ] , sandbagging [ van-der-Weij2024-hk , Tice2025-ke ] , benchmark contamination [ Li2024-jj , Li2023-ux , Yang2023-co , Xu2024-sl , Deng2024-ft , Yao2024-ql ] , and prompt sensitivity [ Sclar2023-ji , Mizrahi...

candidate_problem: Computational inefficiency in Bayesian posterior analysis of semidirected networks due to NP-hard distances
evidence_spans: For semidirected networks with a single root (of unknown placement) [ 10 ] proposed the cut-edge-transfer distance, but it is NP-hard to compute.
problem_evidence_spans: For semidirected networks with a single root (of unknown placement) [ 10 ] proposed the cut-edge-transfer distance, but it is NP-hard to compute.
feasibility_evidence_spans: This μ-representation leads to a dissimilarity measure between semidirected networks of polynomial computational cost
auto_research_experiment: Integrate the proposed mu-distance into a clustering algorithm (e.g., K-medoids) to analyze posterior samples of semidirected networks and compare the time-to-convergence and cluster quality against the cut-edge-transfer distance.

story_angle: Speeding up the study of evolution: A new polynomial-time metric allows researchers to analyze thousands of complex genetic networks in seconds rather than days.

INTRODUCTION: Safety evaluation for large language models proceeds by scoring responses to benchmark prompts and treating those scores as evidence about deployment behavior.
This workflow presumes context robustness: behavior elicited by benchmark-style prompts is informative about behavior under analogous requests in ordinary use.
If benchmark framing changes behavior, scores become entangled with the prompt’s testing context .

Several literatures make this concern plausible: situational-awareness [ Berglund2023-jp , Laine2024-xs ] , alignment-faking and reward-tampering [ Greenblatt2024-di , Denison2024-yr , Hubinger2024-it , Meinke2024-vm , Park2023-nh , Scheurer2023-lr , Hagendorff2024-io ] , sandbagging [ van-der-Weij2024-hk , Tice2025-ke ] , benchmark contamination [ Li2024-jj , Li2023-ux , Yang2023-co , Xu2024-sl , Deng2024-ft , Yao2024-ql ] , and prompt sensitivity [ Sclar2023-ji , Mizrahi...
problem_evidence_spans: Within OLMo-3, the eval-cautious effect is the post-training inversion of a deployment-cautious base prior; across families this inversion does not generalize, and the cross-family heterogeneity is judge-dependent under our cross-judge sensitivity analysis.

candidate_problem: Euclidean distance-based connectivity fails to account for landscape resistance and barriers in species dispersal modeling.
evidence_spans: The model can be improved by incorporating more functional definitions of connectivity. Indeed, we defined connectivity as a function of Euclidean distance between sites.
Integrating a resistance-based formulation of connectivity would be particularly relevant for species like the otter which primarily disperse along rivers and coastlines
highways and large rivers have been shown to limit distributions for many large carnivores
problem_evidence_spans: Euclidean distance alone cannot capture... highways and large rivers have been shown to limit distributions
feasibility_evidence_spans: Euclidean distance is often replaced in connectivity analyses by functional metrics like least-cost path (Coulon et al., 2015; Sutherland et al., 2015) or commute-time (McRae et al., 2008; Kervellec et al., 2024) distances
auto_research_experiment: Replace the Euclidean distance kernel in the provided dynamic occupancy model with a least-cost path (LCP) distance kernel derived from a landscape resistance surface (e.g., land cover, road density).
available_data_or_benchmark: French National Wolf Monitoring data (Louvrier et al. 2018) and Corine Land Cover (CLC) for resistance surfaces.
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/oliviergimenez/wolf_occ_monit (Example of similar wolf occupancy data/code)
scientific_hypothesis: A resistance-based connectivity model will show higher predictive accuracy for wolf range expansion than a Euclidean model by correctly identifying highways as dispersal barriers.
expected_metric: WAIC (Watanabe-Akaike Information Criterion) or AUC on out-of-sample temporal colonization events.
success_threshold: A reduction in WAIC > 10 compared to the Euclidean baseline.
failure_condition: The LCP model shows no improvement in WAIC or results in biologically implausible dispersal parameters (sigma).
first_24h_experiment: Download the Corine Land Cover dataset for France, generate a 10km x 10km resistance grid where 'Highways' have 100x resistance, and compute a sparse LCP distance matrix for the wolf study area.
specific_intervention: Implementation of a Least-Cost Path (LCP) distance algorithm within the sparse matrix framework of the dynamic occupancy model.
baseline: The Euclidean distance-based dynamic occupancy model described in the paper.
story_angle: Moving from 'as the crow flies' to 'as the wolf runs'—improving ecological forecasting by mapping the actual friction of the landscape.

candidate_problem: Test whether Evaluate performance by encouraging exploration and exploitation in the LatentRAG: Latent Reasoning and Retrieval for Efficient Agentic RAG setup addresses a stated future work in LatentRAG: Latent Reasoning and Retrieval for Efficient Agentic RAG.
evidence_spans: Future work could investigate reinforcement learning to improve performance by encouraging exploration and exploitation.
INTRODUCTION: Figure 1: Comparison of performance and latency on multi-hop QA datasets.

As shown in Fig. 1 , on multi-hop question answering (QA) datasets, the total inference time of a representative agentic RAG method, Search-R1 [ 24 ] , requires 16–22 × \times the inference time of naive RAG.

Extensive experiments on seven benchmark datasets show that LatentRAG achieves performance comparable to explicit agentic RAG methods, with relative performance differences of less than 5%, while significantly reducing latency overhead by approximately 90% on average, approaching the latency of traditional single-step RAG.

candidate_problem: Environmental relationships inferred from dispersal-based models are biased by the 'accessible' subset of the landscape, leading to poor extrapolation in highly altered environments.
evidence_spans: A limitation of our approach is that environmental effects are estimated only within the subset of accessible sites
the model may fail to capture relationships that occur outside the range of observed conditions, including potential nonlinear responses.
this highlights that environmental effects inferred from dispersal-based models should be interpreted with caution
problem_evidence_spans: environmental gradients are only partially sampled, the model may fail to capture relationships that occur outside the range of observed conditions
feasibility_evidence_spans: This limitation is illustrated by the effect of urbanization on otters, for which the model estimates a positive association... However, these areas are unlikely to be suitable
auto_research_experiment: Integrate a 'pseudo-absence' or 'background' habitat suitability prior derived from static SDMs (Species Distribution Models) into the dynamic occupancy model to regularize environmental coefficients.
available_data_or_benchmark: Eurasian otter (Lutra lutra) occurrence data in France and Global Biodiversity Information Facility (GBIF) records for static priors.
public_training_or_eval_data: https://www.gbif.org/species/2433164 (GBIF Lutra lutra data)
scientific_hypothesis: Incorporating a global habitat suitability prior will prevent the model from incorrectly predicting high occupancy for otters in hyper-urbanized areas like Paris.
expected_metric: Predictive Mean Squared Error (PMSE) on known absence sites in highly urbanized regions.
success_threshold: Significant reduction in predicted occupancy probability (from >0.5 to <0.2) for the Paris metropolitan area while maintaining accuracy in the expansion front.
failure_condition: The prior overwhelms the dispersal signal, leading to a model that fails to predict the actual expansion front accurately.
first_24h_experiment: Fit a standard MaxEnt model using GBIF data for otters across Europe to create a 'suitability' covariate, then compare its distribution to the paper's 'installation probability' map.
specific_intervention: Bayesian informative priors for the environmental coefficients (alpha_xi) based on a pre-trained static SDM.
baseline: The paper's dynamic occupancy model with flat/uninformative priors.
metric: Occupancy probability in known unsuitable urban sinks.
story_angle: Correcting the 'urban otter' paradox: why range-expanding species seem to love cities (but actually don't).

candidate_problem: Inaccurate effective population size (Ne) estimation in recent generations due to the lack of a unified framework for Identity-By-Descent (IBD) and Runs of Homozygosity (ROH).
evidence_spans: Connecting IBD tracts and runs of homozygosity: A coalescent framework for inferring effective population size
problem_evidence_spans: Connecting IBD tracts and runs of homozygosity
feasibility_evidence_spans: A coalescent framework for inferring effective population size
auto_research_experiment: Implement the proposed coalescent framework to jointly model IBD and ROH distributions and compare Ne recovery against methods using only one signal type.
available_data_or_benchmark: 1000 Genomes Project Phase 3 or UK Biobank genomic data.
public_training_or_eval_data: https://www.internationalgenome.org/data
scientific_hypothesis: Jointly modeling the length distribution of IBD tracts and ROH segments within a single coalescent framework significantly reduces the variance of Ne estimates for the last 10-100 generations.
expected_metric: Mean Squared Error (MSE) of Ne estimation across simulated demographic histories.
success_threshold: A 20% reduction in MSE compared to IBD-only methods like IBDNe.
failure_condition: The joint model fails to converge or provides biased estimates when population structure (admixture) is present but not modeled.
first_24h_experiment: Simulate a population with a known bottleneck using msprime; extract IBD and ROH segments; run a prototype of the joint likelihood estimator to see if it recovers the bottleneck timing.
specific_intervention: Integration of ROH segment lengths into the IBD-based coalescent likelihood function.
baseline: IBDNe (Browning and Browning)
story_angle: By unifying how we look at shared DNA within and between people, we can more accurately reconstruct the recent history of human populations.

candidate_problem: Heterogeneous fitness in template-directed replication destroys pre-replicative polymerization equilibria
evidence_spans: heterogeneous fitness removes it as an equilibrium of the replicated system
differential replication is not just a perturbation of the kinetic background, it creates a genuinely new dynamical scenario
unless all fitness values coincide with the equilibrium average fitness, the replicated vector field does not vanish at the old equilibrium
problem_evidence_spans: heterogeneous fitness removes it as an equilibrium of the replicated system
feasibility_evidence_spans: The log-ratio identities make this transition especially transparent
Simulations of finite truncations show exponential decay of the Lyapunov error toward the stable equilibrium
auto_research_experiment: Simulate a high-dimensional m-ary string space (m=4 for RNA) using the provided ODE framework to identify the 'replication intensity' threshold where kinetic dominance is lost to selective sweeps across different fitness landscapes.
available_data_or_benchmark: The paper provides a deterministic sequence-resolved model of open polymerization on the genealogy of finite m-ary strings.
public_training_or_eval_data: The model uses synthetic m-ary string genealogies; parameters can be grounded in experimental RNA degradation/extension rates from datasets like the RNA Folding Form (RFF) or NUPACK simulations.
scientific_hypothesis: There exists a critical replication intensity R_c, dependent on the sequence depth and kinetic forcing, below which the polymerization-driven distribution remains metastable despite fitness advantages.
expected_metric: Log-ratio divergence rate of fitter vs. kinetic-favored sequences.
success_threshold: Identification of a phase transition boundary in the (R, fitness_gap) plane where the steady-state distribution shifts by >50% in Total Variation distance.
failure_condition: The system remains dominated by the kinetic forcing (precursor input) regardless of replication intensity due to high degradation rates.
first_24h_experiment: Implement the 3D 'core system' ODEs in Python and sweep the replication intensity parameter R to observe the bifurcation from the stable upper resource branch C+.
specific_intervention: Vary the replication intensity R and the fitness gap between sequence lineages in a simulated 4-ary string genealogy.
baseline: The neutral fitness case where replication intensity R=0 (pure polymerization-competition equilibrium).
metric: Total Variation (TV) distance between the steady-state sequence distribution and the pre-replicative equilibrium.

candidate_problem: Inferred phylogenetic networks from LCA-constraints lack biological constraints and structural simplicity
evidence_spans: the networks constructed in this framework are not required to belong to any restricted network class and may therefore be structurally complex.

future work will focus on understanding when a given pair (R, F) can be realized by more specific and biologically constrained classes of phylogenetic networks, such as normal networks [ 32 , 20 ] , level- 1 1 networks [ 7 , 13 ] , or galled trees [ 10 ] .
problem_evidence_spans: the networks constructed in this framework are not required to belong to any restricted network class and may therefore be structurally complex.
feasibility_evidence_spans: In the affirmative case, a phylogenetic network that realizes all constraints in R R while avoiding those in F F can be constructed in polynomial time.
auto_research_experiment: Modify the existing polynomial-time construction algorithm to incorporate a 'level-k' constraint, where each biconnected component has at most k reticulation nodes, and test if the (R, F) realization remains solvable in polynomial time for k=1.
available_data_or_benchmark: The 'PhyloNet' software suite and the 'Tree of Life' datasets (e.g., Open Tree of Life) provide real-world taxa sets to generate R and F constraints.
public_training_or_eval_data: https://opentreeoflife.org/
scientific_hypothesis: A level-1 phylogenetic network (galled tree) can realize a set of allowed (R) and forbidden (F) LCA-constraints if and only if the constraints do not induce a cycle in the condensed taxon-relation graph and satisfy a restricted version of the (Y1)/(Y2) conditions.
expected_metric: Network Complexity (Reticulation Number)
success_threshold: The algorithm produces a level-1 network that satisfies all R and F constraints for at least 80% of biologically derived constraint sets.
failure_condition: The algorithm fails to find a level-1 network even when one exists, or the problem is proven to be NP-hard for level-1 networks.
first_24h_experiment: Implement a brute-force search for level-1 networks on a small set of taxa (n=6) with a random set of 5 allowed and 2 forbidden LCA-constraints to identify the smallest counter-example where the current paper's algorithm produces a complex network but a level-1 network is possible.
specific_intervention: Constraint the BUILD-like algorithm to only merge components that maintain a level-1 topology (no overlapping cycles).
baseline: The polynomial-time algorithm described in the paper (Ebert & Hellmuth, 2026) which constructs unrestricted DAGs.
metric: Reticulation index (number of nodes with in-degree > 1)
story_angle: Moving from mathematical possibility to biological reality: refining phylogenetic network inference to produce 'galled trees' that biologists can actually interpret.

problem_evidence_spans: interactive and embeddable phylogenetic tree visualization

public_training_or_eval_data: https://tree.opentreeoflife.org/

candidate_problem: Lack of formal computational complexity characterizations for price discovery in distributed social systems
evidence_spans: complexity characterizations of price discovery in distributed systems, extending previous works in settings of centralized computation [ 177 , 151 , 47 , 167 ]

a unified framework connecting microscopic social interactions to robust macroscopic behavior has so far proven elusive; on the whole, the application of formal models and theories to the study of human social systems remains fragmented and ad hoc
problem_evidence_spans: a unified framework connecting microscopic social interactions to robust macroscopic behavior has so far proven elusive; on the whole, the application of formal models and theories to the study of human social systems remains fragmented and ad hoc
feasibility_evidence_spans: extending previous works in settings of centralized computation [ 177 , 151 , 47 , 167 ]
auto_research_experiment: Model a market as a distributed graph where nodes only have local information and use a gossip-based protocol to reach a price equilibrium. Measure the communication complexity (bits exchanged) and time to convergence compared to centralized Walrasian auctioneer models.
available_data_or_benchmark: The Penn-Lehigh-Lafayette (PLL) dataset or the simulated 'Trading Agent Competition' (TAC) market environments.
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/j-p-m-g/trading-agent-competition
scientific_hypothesis: Distributed price discovery in a network with limited connectivity has a communication complexity that scales quadratically with the number of agents, unlike the linear scaling assumed in centralized models.
expected_metric: Communication complexity (total bits) and Convergence Time (steps).
success_threshold: The model predicts convergence time within 15% of empirical results from experimental game theory datasets.
failure_condition: The distributed model fails to reach equilibrium or the complexity remains identical to centralized models regardless of network topology.
first_24h_experiment: Implement a simple distributed price discovery algorithm (e.g., Tatonnement on a graph) using the Mesa agent-based modeling framework and measure convergence time across different graph densities.

baseline: Centralized Walrasian Auctioneer model (global information aggregation).
story_angle: We are treating the global economy not as a vague 'invisible hand' but as a concrete distributed computer, measuring exactly how much 'bandwidth' a society needs to function.

candidate_problem: Inability to quantify the 'computational power' gained by societal restructuring and hierarchy during scaling
evidence_spans: arguably, these restructurings provide greater computational power, enabling societies to solve coordination problems at previously unimaginable scales [ 105 , 184 ]
What has not traditionally been examined is the computational nature of these social processes: the resources they require, how hard they are to solve or even approximate
problem_evidence_spans: What has not traditionally been examined is the computational nature of these social processes: the resources they require, how hard they are to solve or even approximate
feasibility_evidence_spans: non-uniform complexity and distributed computing formalize constraints and structure of problem solving in real world architectures, such as Boolean circuits, capturing modularity and hierarchy
auto_research_experiment: Map historical social hierarchies (from the Seshat Global History Databank) to Boolean circuit depth and width. Test if the 'computational depth' of a society correlates with its ability to manage complex resource allocation tasks.
available_data_or_benchmark: Seshat: Global History Databank.
public_training_or_eval_data: https://seshatdatabank.info/data/
scientific_hypothesis: Societal transition from egalitarian to hierarchical structures corresponds to a transition from shallow, wide circuits to deep, modular circuits, optimizing for communication latency over raw processing power.
expected_metric: Circuit Depth (hierarchy levels) and Structural Complexity Index.
success_threshold: A statistically significant correlation (p < 0.01) between Seshat complexity scores and the calculated circuit depth of the administrative hierarchy.
failure_condition: No correlation found between administrative levels and the complexity of tasks the society successfully managed.
first_24h_experiment: Download Seshat 'Social Complexity' variables and perform a preliminary mapping of 'Levels of Hierarchy' to a circuit depth model for 10 diverse historical polities.

baseline: Standard allometric scaling models (e.g., Bettencourt's urban scaling).
metric: Information processing capacity (bits per unit time).
story_angle: By viewing ancient empires as biological computers, we can finally explain why they 'crashed' when their administrative circuits became too deep to handle noise.

classification errors are driven by both class imbalance and intrinsic biological similarity

public_training_or_eval_data: TCGA-BRCA via GDC Data Portal.

story_angle: Stop picking genes just because they are 'loud' (high variance); pick them because they are 'meaningful' (biological pathways).

candidate_problem: Intractability of the Branching Schrödinger Bridge (BSB) semi-coupling and RUOT relaxation
evidence_spans: A limitation of USB is that the semi-coupling of BSB and its RUOT relaxation are intractable. Consequently, we use WFR for approximation. The underlying mathematics merits further investigation.
problem_evidence_spans: A limitation of USB is that the semi-coupling of BSB and its RUOT relaxation are intractable.
feasibility_evidence_spans: We validated the effectiveness and robustness of the method on both synthetic and real scRNA-seq datasets
Algorithm 2 USB training workflow
auto_research_experiment: Develop a neural approximation of the BSB semi-coupling using a variational objective that minimizes the KL divergence between the WFR-based path and the true branching process, then compare against the current USB approximation.
available_data_or_benchmark: scRNA-seq datasets (e.g., Waddington-OT datasets or Schiebinger et al. 2019)
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/broadinstitute/wot
scientific_hypothesis: A learned neural semi-coupling for BSB will yield more accurate discrete cell-count predictions than the WFR approximation in systems with high proliferation rates.
expected_metric: Earth Mover's Distance (EMD) between predicted and ground-truth cell distributions at snapshot $t_k$.
success_threshold: 15% reduction in EMD compared to the WFR-based USB baseline.
failure_condition: The neural semi-coupling fails to converge or produces higher EMD than the static WFR approximation.
first_24h_experiment: Implement a 1D synthetic branching process (e.g., a bifurcating Gaussian) and compare the USB (WFR-based) marginals against the exact analytical BSB marginals.
specific_intervention: Replace the RUOT/WFR static coupling in Algorithm 2, Step 3, with a learned variational coupling specific to the BSB problem.
baseline: USB with WFR approximation (as described in the paper).
story_angle: Bridging the gap between continuous flow matching and discrete biological branching through exact mathematical couplings.

Such empirical designs of communication structures have negative impacts on downstream performance of LLM-MAS, e.g., efficiency, accuracy, stability, and robustness.

Therefore, a small set of randomly selected training tasks may not well represent the overall task pool, potentially reducing the robustness of the learned structure and leading to unreliable downstream accuracy.

evidence_spans: Not so well represented was the shape of the oscillations. In particular, the M phases were much shorter than the S phases.
problem_evidence_spans: Not so well represented was the shape of the oscillations. In particular, the M phases were much shorter than the S phases.
feasibility_evidence_spans: One can hope that this could be changed by modifications of other model parameters.
The raw model consists of six ordinary differential equations depending on fifteen parameters and three Hill coefficients.
auto_research_experiment: Perform a global sensitivity analysis on the 15-parameter ODE system to identify which parameters (e.g., Hill coefficients or phosphatase rates) control the M-phase duration relative to the S-phase, then use a multi-objective genetic algorithm to fit the model to the experimental waveform shapes from Deneke et al. (2016).

baseline: The original 3-ODE reduced model presented in the paper (Mebratie et al., 2026).

candidate_problem: Docking failure of AlphaFold-predicted chain models into cryo-EM density maps
evidence_spans: Future efforts will aim to refine this pipeline, particularly the docking step, where the chain models occasionally failed to dock correctly in the map.
problem_evidence_spans: Future efforts will aim to refine this pipeline, particularly the docking step, where the chain models occasionally failed to dock correctly in the map.
feasibility_evidence_spans: Specifically, we employ AlphaFold-predicted structures to segment certain map regions rich in interpretative and informational content
This structure is the phosphorylated, ATP-bound structure of zebrafish cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator, with an EMDB ID: 8782 and a PDB ID: 5W81.
auto_research_experiment: Implement a flexible fitting or multi-state docking algorithm (e.g., using Rosetta or molecular dynamics flexible fitting) to replace the current docking step in PhenixCraft and evaluate on the 20-map test set.
available_data_or_benchmark: The 20 EMDB/PDB pairs listed in Table S1 (e.g., EMDB 8782/PDB 5W81, EMDB 8194/PDB 5K12).
public_training_or_eval_data: EMDataResource (EMDB) and Protein Data Bank (PDB).
scientific_hypothesis: Replacing rigid docking with a gradient-based flexible fitting of AlphaFold models into the segmented density will reduce docking failures and increase the final TM-score.
expected_metric: TM-score
success_threshold: An average TM-score increase of >0.05 across the 20 test maps compared to the baseline PhenixCraft.
failure_condition: The flexible fitting introduces distortions that lower the sequence match percentage or fail to converge within 2 hours per map.
first_24h_experiment: Download EMDB 8782 and its corresponding AlphaFold model; attempt to dock using the existing Phenix 'fit_relative_to_ref' and compare to a flexible fitting tool like 'phenix.real_space_refine'.
specific_intervention: Integration of a flexible fitting algorithm (e.g., Namdinator or Phenix real-space refinement) into the PhenixCraft docking module.
baseline: The original PhenixCraft pipeline (AlphaFold-based segmentation + standard Phenix docking).
story_angle: Fixing the 'last mile' of cryo-EM model building by making foundation model predictions flexible enough to fit real-world experimental noise.

candidate_problem: Low sequence accuracy in low-resolution ( >4.0 Å) cryo-EM density maps
feasibility_evidence_spans: Table S1: A list of the TM-scores and sequence match percentages by PhenixCraft and phenix.map_to_model for 20 constructed atomic models
auto_research_experiment: Apply a sequence-aware threading algorithm (like RosettaES) specifically to the regions segmented by PhenixCraft to improve side-chain placement in low-resolution maps.
available_data_or_benchmark: The low-resolution subset of Table S1 (EMDB 6724, 6679, 3240).
public_training_or_eval_data: EMDB and PDB.
scientific_hypothesis: Using AlphaFold's predicted side-chain orientations as a prior during the Phenix refinement stage will improve sequence match % in maps >4.0 Å.
expected_metric: Sequence match %
success_threshold: A 10% absolute increase in sequence match percentage for maps with resolution >4.0 Å.
failure_condition: The AlphaFold side-chain priors bias the model toward the predicted structure rather than the experimental density, leading to lower R-factors.
first_24h_experiment: Run the PhenixCraft pipeline on EMDB 6724 and manually inspect if the sequence mismatch occurs in the AlphaFold-segmented regions.
specific_intervention: Side-chain rotamer optimization using AlphaFold-derived probability distributions during the Phenix refinement step.
baseline: PhenixCraft (current version).
story_angle: Overcoming the 'resolution barrier' in cryo-EM by using AI priors to place amino acids where the map is too blurry to see them.

candidate_problem: High-dimensional sparsity and noise in EHR-based CRS prediction leads to overfitting and computational inefficiency when using raw OMOP event logs.
evidence_spans: But coded clinical features are extremely high-dimensional, sparse, and dominated by weakly informative variables.
This setting is often ill-posed relative to sample size, introducing noise and computational burdens that degrade predictive performance and increase the risk of overfitting.
problem_evidence_spans: This setting is often ill-posed relative to sample size, introducing noise and computational burdens that degrade predictive performance and increase the risk of overfitting.
feasibility_evidence_spans: We utilized a nationwide longitudinal EHR dataset from the All of Us Research Program
We introduce a hybrid feature selection framework that compresses more than 110,000 clinical codes to 100 key features.
auto_research_experiment: Compare the paper's hybrid feature selection (statistical prevalence + model-based importance) against a Deep Learning-based feature selection method (e.g., TabNet or LassoNet) on the All of Us dataset to see if automated selection can further reduce noise without losing predictive AUC.
available_data_or_benchmark: All of Us Research Program (AoU) Researcher Workbench
public_training_or_eval_data: https://www.researchallofus.org/data-tools/workbench/
scientific_hypothesis: Automated deep feature selection using LassoNet will outperform hybrid statistical/XGBoost selection by capturing non-linear interactions between sparse clinical codes that are missed by prevalence-based screening.
expected_metric: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC)
success_threshold: AUC > 0.85 (improvement of >0.004 over the paper's 0.8461)
failure_condition: Deep selection methods fail to converge or underperform due to the extreme sparsity of the 110,000+ initial features compared to the statistical screening.
first_24h_experiment: Access the AoU Workbench, extract the cohort of CRS cases (ICD-10 J32) and controls, and run a baseline LassoNet on a 10% sample of the 110k features.
specific_intervention: Replace the hybrid statistical/XGBoost feature selection pipeline with a LassoNet (end-to-end feature selection) architecture.
baseline: The paper's hybrid selection + XGBoost pipeline (AUC 0.8461).
story_angle: Moving from manual statistical screening to automated deep feature selection in high-dimensional clinical data.

candidate_problem: Demographic-specific models improve performance but may introduce 'fairness gaps' or disparate impact if certain subgroups (e.g., younger males) have significantly lower recall than others.
evidence_spans: The grouped strategy improved weighted AUC by 0.0168 to 0.8461, with gains in every stratum.
where males showed an age-dependent shift toward predominantly structural and polypoid pathology, whereas females exhibited profiles enriched for mucosal inflammation
problem_evidence_spans: Our approach demonstrates improved discrimination both overall and across subgroups.
feasibility_evidence_spans: Specifically, individuals were stratified into six subgroups defined by the intersection of sex and age-based adult life stages.
auto_research_experiment: Evaluate the fairness of the stratified XGBoost models by calculating the Equality of Opportunity metric (FNR parity) across the six demographic subgroups.
available_data_or_benchmark: All of Us Research Program (AoU) Researcher Workbench
public_training_or_eval_data: https://www.researchallofus.org/data-tools/workbench/
scientific_hypothesis: Stratified modeling reduces overall error but increases the disparity in False Negative Rates between sex-age groups compared to a single model with demographic fairness constraints.
expected_metric: False Negative Rate (FNR) Disparity
success_threshold: A reduction in FNR disparity by 20% while maintaining the paper's AUC.
failure_condition: Fairness constraints significantly degrade the AUC below the 0.8293 baseline.
first_24h_experiment: Calculate the FNR for each of the 6 subgroups using the paper's stratification logic on the AoU dataset.
specific_intervention: Apply Fairlearn's Exponentiated Gradient reduction to the XGBoost training process to enforce FNR parity across the 6 strata.
baseline: The paper's stratified XGBoost models without fairness constraints.
story_angle: Ensuring that AI-driven CRS diagnosis doesn't leave specific demographic groups behind.
